articles.title: [Editorial] The next steps for chronic kidney disease
articles.authors: The Lancet
articles.summary: Chronic kidney disease (CKD) has emerged after decades of neglect, we noted in November 2025, with the adoption of the WHO resolution on kidney health. The resolution was a long overdue recognition of the need for concerted global attention. However, efforts to implement and operationalise these kinds of agreements are often met with opposition, inertia, and indifference. A Health Policy paper in this week's issue of The Lancet provides practical guidance on how to fulfil the resolution's promises, drawing on lessons from diabetes control and calling for integration in the broader non-communicable disease agenda.

articles.title: [Comment] Highlights 2026 photography competition: health and humanity in focus
articles.authors: Joanna Palmer
articles.summary: The Lancet's annual Highlights photography competition began in 2009; since then we have published photographs that capture a rich variety of health stories worldwide. In 2025 some of the winning images came from China, India, Kenya, Japan, Mali, Nepal, South Africa, South Korea, The Gambia, the USA, and Zimbabwe.1 Highlights 2026 is now open for submissions.

articles.title: [Comment] The Wakley Prize 2026: learning experience
articles.authors: Niall Boyce, Chloe Wilson, Joanna Palmer, Maneet Virdi, Madeleine Clarke, Claire Lenahan, Electra Way, Kate Wilcock
articles.summary: “In medicine, needless to say, the moment you feel as if you've mastered something is invariably the point at which your next experience will knock you straight back down to earth.”1 Physician and writer Rachel Clarke's words will ring true, painfully so, with anyone in practice, but especially early-career clinicians. Doctors emerge proudly from final examinations and assessments, brains overflowing with facts and figures, only to realise that the real process of learning has only just begun. For this year's Wakley Prize, we're interested in the early years of a health professional's career.

articles.title: [World Report] WHO and Africa CDC launch $518 million Ebola plan

articles.summary: The organisations’ announcement promises a unified coordinated response to controlling the Bundibugyo Ebola virus outbreak. John Zarocostas reports.

articles.title: [World Report] Petition calls for boycott of Israeli Medical Association

articles.summary: Critics are calling for the IMA to be suspended from the World Medical Association over its stance on Gaza. Udani Samarasekera reports.

articles.title: [World Report] The medicine of…cruise ships
articles.authors: Talha Burki
articles.summary: Although the hantavirus outbreak on MV Hondius has drawn the world's attention, day-to-day practice is usually a little more prosaic. Talha Burki embarks on an examination of cruise ships.

articles.title: [Perspectives] Art on repeat prescription
articles.authors: Kate Womersley
articles.summary: At times of stress, we are often told to do less. But a crammed diary has always been a cue for me to do more: snatch time to read, pick up a pen, paint with my daughter. These activities soothe and stretch my brain in ways I can’t find otherwise, although I have never thought of them as contributions to my health; rather, part of how I live. It is concerning that doctors are less likely than the general population to engage in the arts because evidence has long highlighted their empathy-enhancing potential.

articles.title: [Perspectives] Between two worlds: war, memory, and what resists within
articles.authors: Hala Kerbage
articles.summary: I am sitting in my office in Montpellier, France, between two consultations, when I glance at my phone and see the notifications accumulating: images of buildings reduced to dust, familiar streets rendered unrecognisable, places I know by heart. Beirut, my home city, is being bombarded again. A few minutes later, I begin a new consultation with a patient, listen, assess, and organise a treatment plan. Outwardly, nothing has changed. And yet, something within me has shifted, in a way that resonates with processes I recognise clinically: a subtle activation of threat-related vigilance and emotional memory.

articles.title: [Obituary] J Craig Venter
articles.authors: Georgina Ferry
articles.summary: Geneticist and entrepreneur who sequenced the first microbial genome and set up a commercial rival to the Human Genome Project. Born in Salt Lake City, UT, USA, on Oct 14, 1946, he died of cancer on April 29, 2026, in San Diego, CA, USA, aged 79 years.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Challenges in assessing statin-associated adverse events
articles.authors: Marte F van der Bijl, Ruben J M Mijnster, P Hugo M van der Kuy, Melvin Lafeber, Jeanine E Roeters van Lennep
articles.summary: With great interest we read the recent meta-analysis by the Cholesterol Treatment Trialists’ (CTT) Collaboration, which provides reassuring evidence regarding common safety concerns associated with statin therapy, using individual participant data from large double-blind trials.1

articles.title: [Correspondence] Challenges in assessing statin-associated adverse events
articles.authors: Alessandro Rovetta, Steven D Stovitz, Leonardo Biscetti, Mohammad Ali Mansournia
articles.summary: The Cholesterol Treatment Trialists’ (CTT) Collaboration report an individual participant data meta-analysis of double-blind randomised controlled trials (RCTs) assessing whether adverse outcomes listed in statin product labels are causally attributable to statin therapy.1 Their analysis, aimed at controlling the false discovery rate at 5%, concluded that the data do not support causal relationships between statin therapy and most labelled conditions listed on product labels as potential undesirable effects.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Challenges in assessing statin-associated adverse events – Authors’ reply
articles.authors: Christina Reith, Jonathan R Emberson, David Preiss, Rory Collins, Colin Baigent, Cholesterol Treatment Trialists’ (CTT) Collaboration
articles.summary: We thank Marte F van der Bijl and colleagues and Alessandro Rovetta and colleagues for their interest in our Article.1 Using data from 123 940 participants in 19 double-blind randomised trials of a statin versus placebo, we assessed the evidence base for claims of treatment-related adverse effects currently listed in statin product labels. For the vast majority of such outcomes, we found no evidence that they were increased (or decreased) by statin therapy. Consequently, we concluded that these trial data do not support causal relationships between statin therapy and most of the conditions listed in product labels as potential adverse effects.

articles.title: [Editorial] Responding to Ebola: a test
articles.authors: The Lancet
articles.summary: The Ebola virus disease outbreak in DR Congo and Uganda is already a severe public health crisis. As The Lancet goes to press, 116 suspected cases, 330 confirmed cases, and 49 deaths have been reported. The true numbers are likely to be larger. WHO Director-General Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus has conceded that the outbreak is already outpacing response. This is a challenging moment for global health: traditional donors have retreated, funding has shrunk, multilateralism is floundering, and fragile health and social systems need to respond under pressure.

articles.title: [World Report] Rik Peeperkorn: health and humanitarianism in Gaza
articles.authors: John Zarocostas
articles.summary: After 5 years as the WHO representative in the occupied Palestinian territory, Rik Peeperkorn reflects on one of the world's most severe humanitarian crises. John Zarocostas reports.

articles.title: [World Report] Hypertension control: a South Korean success story
articles.authors: Faith McLellan
articles.summary: South Korea is one of the few countries considered to have controlled hypertension thanks to concerted policy over the past 15 years. Faith McLellan reports.

articles.title: [Perspectives] Donald Warne: redressing inequities in Indigenous health
articles.authors: Aarathi Prasad
articles.summary: “I love primary care medicine, but my frustration with it was that almost everything I was dealing with was preventable”, recalls Donald Warne, Professor in International Health and Co-Director of the Johns Hopkins Center for Indigenous Health, Baltimore, MD, USA. In 1995, Warne, who comes from the Oglala Lakota tribe, South Dakota, had graduated with a medical degree from Stanford University. In 2002, he went on to complete a master's in public health at Harvard University, with a focus on health policy.

articles.title: [Perspectives] Still dying: adverse birth outcomes among Black people in the USA
articles.authors: Mary T Bassett
articles.summary: Khiara Bridges, a lawyer and anthropologist, has long wrestled with inequities in pregnancy outcomes between Black and White people. In her first book on the topic, Reproducing Race: An Ethnography of Pregnancy as a Site of Racialization in 2011, Bridges proved a careful and astute observer. She uncovered how advanced obstetric care for US Medicaid recipients (the government health insurance for low-income individuals) or uninsured people was often delivered with implicit contempt and stereotyping.

articles.title: [Perspectives] The way forward
articles.authors: Marco De Ambrogi
articles.summary: Although school shootings remain rare, each time one is reported in the media it is met with shock and disbelief. Over the past two decades, school shootings have increased in the USA and have also happened in other countries, leaving schools, families, and communities with long-lasting trauma. Understanding the causes of a school shooting is important, but another aspect that is often overlooked is the previous behaviour of the shooter and what the people around them might have done to prevent the devastating murder of innocent teenagers.

articles.title: [Perspectives] Ethical shunts: an old woman in an Italian emergency department
articles.authors: Mirko Pasquini, Theodore Michaels, Scott D Stonington
articles.summary: Silvana was an 82-year-old woman living in a nursing home in northern Italy with right-sided weakness and aphasia after a stroke. One evening the medical staff found her on the floor next to her bed, looking pale, mildly short of breath, less responsive than usual, and unable to report what had happened. The staff had a vague sense that she had been declining for months and could, at any moment, develop a life-threatening condition. The staff called an ambulance and told the emergency medical service workers that they thought Silvana might have fallen.

articles.title: [Obituary] Harold Ellis
articles.authors: Jacqui Thornton
articles.summary: Surgeon, exceptional educator, and expert in clinical anatomy. Born on Jan 13, 1926, in east London, UK, he died in London, UK, on March 25, 2026, aged 100 years.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Time to integrate pets in One Health surveillance
articles.authors: Christian Sonne
articles.summary: The increasing risks to human health and society from the triple planetary crisis (ie, climate change, loss of biodiversity, and pollution) underscore the need for a holistic One Health framework to safeguard sustainability and planetary health.1,2 Specifically, exposure to toxic industrial chemicals and emerging infectious diseases, including zoonoses, points towards companion animals—particularly dogs such as circum-Arctic sled dogs, as well as cats and birds—sharing the human exposome, making them excellent early-warning sentinels.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Minocycline for acute ischaemic stroke: limitations of the EMPHASIS trial
articles.authors: Sherief Ghozy, Alzhraa S Abbas, Ramanathan Kadirvel, Alejandro A Rabinstein, David F Kallmes
articles.summary: The EMPHASIS trial reports a modest increase in 90-day functional outcomes with a 4·5-day minocycline course.1 Although this study helpfully re-examines neuroprotection in the reperfusion era, several design features temper confidence in its immediate generalisability.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Minocycline for acute ischaemic stroke: limitations of the EMPHASIS trial – Authors’ reply
articles.authors: Yao Lu, Yilong Wang
articles.summary: We thank Sherief Ghozy and colleagues for their comments on the EMPHASIS trial.1 We agree that neuroprotective strategies in acute ischaemic stroke should be interpreted in the context of statistical robustness, background therapy, patient selection, and generalisability.

articles.title: RFX7 is a tumor suppressor that restricts MYC function and B cell activation
articles.summary: Nature Immunology, Published online: 02 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41590-026-02533-3Our results show that the transcription factor RFX7 acts as a tumor suppressor in mouse models of B cell lymphoma. RFX7 limits the activity of MYC, a key regulator of B cell activation and lymphomagenesis. We also demonstrate that alterations of RFX7 found in lymphomas can substantially affect its transcriptional activity and patient prognosis.

articles.title: [Editorial] A new era in neurology
articles.authors: The Lancet
articles.summary: Neurological disorders are now the leading cause of disability worldwide, with profound consequences for patients, caregivers, and health-care systems. As populations age, the global burden of neurological disease will rise substantially. Yet neurology has transformed over the past two decades. Advances in genetics, molecular imaging, and biomarkers are shifting the field from being syndromic to biologically defined, reshaping disease management. Immunotherapies and gene-based approaches are beginning to modify the natural course of diseases previously thought untreatable.

articles.title: [Comment] Integrating peripheral immune control and brain health in the multiple sclerosis continuum
articles.authors: Eleonora Cocco
articles.summary: Halting the insidious accumulation of irreversible disability remains the principal challenge in multiple sclerosis care. Once classified as relapsing (including relapsing-remitting and active secondary progressive) and primary progressive (PPMS) forms, multiple sclerosis is now recognised as a continuous biological spectrum in which acute peripheral inflammation and CNS-intrinsic neurodegeneration co-occur from onset.1 Progression is primarily driven by compartmentalised neuroinflammation, such as meningeal B-cell follicles and chronically activated microglia, that strongly shapes the pathogenesis of progressive forms.

articles.title: [Comment] Plasma biomarkers for Alzheimer's disease among middle-aged individuals
articles.authors: Anna Rosenberg, Tiia Ngandu
articles.summary: Alzheimer's disease has a long preclinical phase in which neuropathological changes, such as amyloid β (Aβ) and tau pathology, accumulate over many years.1 This preclinical phase provides an opportunity for earlier disease detection and interventions to prevent or delay dementia. Emerging blood-based biomarkers provide a minimally invasive approach to assess Alzheimer's disease-related pathology and show promising diagnostic and prognostic value in symptomatic individuals.2 However, performance of these biomarkers in cognitively unimpaired, middle-aged populations remains unclear.

articles.title: [Comment] Advancing tau-PET imaging in Alzheimer's disease
articles.authors: Joseph Therriault
articles.summary: The development of PET radioligands for the quantification of amyloid-β (Aβ) plaques over 20 years ago1 contributed substantially to Alzheimer's disease research, drug development, and clinical care. 10 years later, the development of radioligands for tau pathology,2 the second proteinopathy in Alzheimer's disease, has had a similar impact on Alzheimer's disease, becoming both an inclusion criterion and an outcome measure for recent Alzheimer's disease clinical trials.

articles.title: [Comment] Prasinezumab: what have we learned from PASADENA and PADOVA?
articles.authors: Thomas Foltynie
articles.summary: The concept of slowing down Parkinson's disease progression by using an antibody targeting aggregating forms of α-synuclein is intuitively very appealing. However, demonstrating efficacy of a potential disease-modifying therapy is highly challenging and a consensus has not been reached on the optimal trial design.1 The PASADENA trial evaluated two different doses of prasinezumab (an antibody that targets the C-terminal of aggregated forms of α-synuclein) given by monthly intravenous infusions in treatment-naive patients or those treated with monoamine oxidase type B (MAO-B) inhibitors.

articles.title: [Comment] Blood biomarkers for Alzheimer's disease: from detection to decisions
articles.authors: Marc Suárez-Calvet, Gemma Salvadó, Beatriu Bilbeny de Fortuny, Marta del Campo
articles.summary: Alzheimer's disease biomarkers have moved from being a focus of research to being used as clinical tools with remarkable speed. This shift is most visible in blood biomarkers, where advances in analytical and diagnostic accuracy now make detection of Alzheimer's pathology widely available to many patients. This progress should be recognised for what it is: a major gain for patients. Blood biomarkers can widen access and align diagnosis with the biology underlying the disease rather than syndromic labels alone.

articles.title: [Comment] Multi-arm multi-stage platform trials for neurological disease: accelerating progress
articles.authors: Paresh Malhotra, Suvankar Pal, Emma Tallantyre, Thomas Foltynie, Camille Carroll, Siddharthan Chandran, James Carpenter, Mahesh Parmar, Susan Scott, Jeremy Chataway
articles.summary: Neurological diseases are the worldwide leading cause of ill health and disability.1 Although substantial progress has been made, such as modulating the inflammatory component of multiple sclerosis,2 neurodegenerative biology remains largely intractable, including in conditions such as Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, and motor neuron disease. Recent scientific advances have deepened our understanding of neurodegenerative pathobiology, enabling targeted identification of mechanistically plausible candidate treatments.

articles.title: [Comment] Offline: The dishonest politics of global health
articles.authors: Richard Horton
articles.summary: The latest outbreak of Ebola in DR Congo loomed over proceedings at the World Health Assembly (WHA) last week. The first case: April 24. A Public Health Emergency of International Concern (PHEIC): May 17. 23 days: unconscionable delay or understandable caution? As Edem Adzogenu, Global Emissary for the Accra Reset, pointed out, Ebola was discovered in DR Congo in 1976—and, 50 years later, there is still no adequate national surveillance capacity. A second issue poisoned many conversations in Geneva: what should a future global health architecture look like? And who should lead its design? Presumably WHO would play an important part.

articles.title: [World Report] From hope to uncertainty: regulating novel drugs for Alzheimer's disease
articles.authors: Nayanah Siva
articles.summary: Why have regulatory agencies offered differing assessments of new treatments that have brought promise to millions of people affected by Alzheimer's disease? Nayanah Siva reports.

articles.title: [World Report] Ebola in Africa
articles.authors: John Agaba
articles.summary: A lack of vaccines, diagnostics, and basic public health capacity is raising fears over the scale of the Ebola outbreak in DR Congo and Uganda. John Agaba reports from Kampala.

articles.title: [Perspectives] Stephen Hauser: transforming treatment for multiple sclerosis
articles.authors: Udani Samarasekera
articles.summary: As a neurology resident at Massachusetts General Hospital (MGH), USA, in the 1970s, Stephen Hauser met Andrea, a critically ill young patient with multiple sclerosis (MS) and acute severe hemiplegia. “I remember thinking this was the most heartbreaking problem that I had ever seen in medicine”, says Hauser, the Robert A Fishman Distinguished Professor in Neurology at the University of California San Francisco (UCSF) and Director of the UCSF Weill Institute for Neurosciences, CA, USA. Andrea's condition and the lack of therapeutic care for her inspired his desire “to contribute to understanding MS, and especially to try to develop treatments”.

articles.title: [Perspectives] Reisa Sperling: getting ahead of Alzheimer's disease
articles.authors: Faith McLellan
articles.summary: Born in Memphis, TN, Reisa Sperling grew up in Bethlehem, PA, USA. She left there aged 17 years to become an actress in New York City, an adventure that lasted for nearly a decade, until she decided she did not want to spend her life “playing small musical animals”, she recalls. Fortunately, she then found her way to Columbia University's School of General Studies, which was created specifically for “non-traditional students”, and where she studied biology and psychology. Torn between a career in psychology or neurology, she realised “if I wanted to actually be able to do something about disease, I should get a medical degree”.

articles.title: [Obituary] Shichuo Li
articles.authors: Talha Burki
articles.summary: Neuroepidemiologist and neurosurgeon who improved epilepsy care in China. He was born in Shahe City, Hebei Province, China, on April 10, 1941, and died on March 7, 2026, in Beijing, China, aged 84 years.

articles.title: [Articles] Efficacy and safety of a bodyweight–adjusted higher dose of ocrelizumab in relapsing (MUSETTE) and primary progressive (GAVOTTE) multiple sclerosis: two multicentre, randomised, double-blind, parallel-group phase 3b trials
articles.authors: Stephen L Hauser, Gavin Giovannoni, Xavier Montalban, Jiwon Oh, Amit Bar-Or, Maria Pia Sormani, Martin S Weber, Sharon Stoll, Jacqueline A Nicholas, Tetyana Nehrych, Robert Bonek, Krzysztof Selmaj, Maciej Maciejowski, Dusanka Zecevic, Catarina Raposo, Rebecca Owen, Ulrike Bonati, Katrin Madjar, Eva Harfst, Qing Wang, Anastasiia Raievska, Marianna Manfrini, Hans-Martin Schneble, Ludwig Kappos, MUSETTE and GAVOTTE Study Groups
articles.summary: In both studies, high-dose ocrelizumab did not further improve control of disability progression in either RMS or PPMS, and no new safety concerns were identified.

articles.title: [Articles] Efficacy and safety of ocrelizumab in primary progressive multiple sclerosis, including older patients and those with more advanced disease (ORATORIO-HAND): a multicentre, double-blind, randomised, placebo-controlled, phase 3b study
articles.authors: Gavin Giovannoni, Laura Airas, Riley Bove, Gary R Cutter, Maciej Czarnecki, Jelena Drulovic, Jeremy Hobart, Jens Kuhle, Xavier Montalban, Krzysztof W Selmaj, Carmen Tur, Jerry S Wolinsky, Anna Baldinotti, Ulrike Bonati, Licinio Craveiro, Caroline Giacobino, Marianna Manfrini, Hans-Martin Schneble, Paula Stevenson, Qing Wang, Ke Yang, Jiwon Oh, ORATORIO-HAND study group
articles.summary: Ocrelizumab was superior to placebo in delaying disability progression, with stronger effect on hand function, in a broad PPMS population including older patients and those with more advanced disease, while maintaining a manageable safety profile.

articles.title: [Articles] Alzheimer's disease neuropathology plasma biomarkers and cognition in midlife: a community-based cohort study
articles.authors: Xiaqing Jiang, Tina D Hoang, Leslie M Shaw, David R Jacobs, Ilya M Nasrallah, R Nick Bryan, Kristine Yaffe
articles.summary: Alzheimer's disease neuropathology is relatively uncommon in midlife but associated with worse cognitive performance and accelerated decline and might have stronger association among some groups. Early Alzheimer's disease neuropathology detection with the use of plasma biomarkers might enable timely prevention and intervention in midlife adults including risk reduction and pharmacological therapies.

articles.title: [Articles] Comparison of [18F]flortaucipir and [18F]MK6240 for the detection of tau pathology in Alzheimer's disease (HEAD): a multicentre, prospective, cross-sectional, within-participant study
articles.authors: Guilherme Povala, Bruna Bellaver, Firoza Z Lussier, Livia Amaral, Guilherme Bauer-Negrini, Pamela C L Ferreira, Andreia Rocha, Emma Ruppert, Marina S Medeiros, Cécile Tissot, William J Jagust, Joseph C Masdeu, Juan Fortea, Dana L Tudorascu, David N Soleimani-Meigooni, Val Lowe, Hwamee Oh, Belen Pascual, Brian A Gordon, Pedro Rosa-Neto, Suzanne Baker, Tharick A Pascoal, on behalf of the HEAD Study
articles.summary: Tau PET tracer selection influences the frequency of detection of tau pathology across the ageing and Alzheimer's disease spectrum. Compared with [18F]flortaucipir, [18F]MK6240 identified more individuals with tau pathology in cognitively unimpaired and cognitively impaired individuals, with direct implications for patient stratification in clinical trials and more precise guidance for therapeutic decision-making.

articles.title: [Articles] Efficacy and safety of intravenous prasinezumab in individuals with early-stage Parkinson's disease on stable symptomatic monotherapy (PADOVA): a phase 2b, multicentre, randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled study
articles.authors: Tania Nikolcheva, Gennaro Pagano, Judith Anzures-Cabrera, Dylan Trundell, Thomas Kustermann, Tanya Simuni, Kenneth Marek, Nicola Pavese, Klaus Seppi, Fabrizio Stocchi, Ronald B Postuma, Nathalie Pross, Annabelle Monnet, Gesine Respondek, Valerie Schlegel, Lauren Boak, Loes Rutten-Jacobs, Geoffrey A Kerchner, Patrik Brundin, Hanno Svoboda, Azad Bonni, PADOVA Investigators, the Prasinezumab Study Group
articles.summary: Although PADOVA did not meet the primary endpoint, prespecified exploratory evidence suggests clinical activity of prasinezumab in early-stage Parkinson's disease, supporting continued investigation in the ongoing phase 3 PARAISO trial (NCT07174310).

articles.title: [Seminar] Alzheimer's disease
articles.authors: Alberto Serrano-Pozo, Valentina Escott-Price, Lea T Grinberg, Tharick Pascoal, Marc Suárez-Calvet, Bruno Dubois, Reisa A Sperling
articles.summary: Alzheimer's disease is the leading cause of dementia and among the top ten leading causes of death in high-income countries. Exponential advances in epidemiology, genetics, diagnostic imaging and fluid biomarkers, treatment, and prevention in the last decade reinforce the notion that we are entering a new era in the clinical management of Alzheimer's disease. However, far from triumphalism, this momentum should be accelerated to achieve the goals of preventing Alzheimer's disease and arresting its progression.

articles.title: Fungal glycans boost mRNA-based vaccines
articles.summary: Nature Immunology, Published online: 25 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41590-026-02522-6mRNA-based vaccines saved millions of lives during the COVID-19 pandemic, but they require frequent boosting and periodic updates to match emerging virus strains. We demonstrate that fungal glycans can function as adjuvants for mRNA-based vaccines, expanding the durability and breath of protection against several virus strains. These findings support the development of a new generation of RNA-based vaccines with improved performance.

articles.title: [Comment] Measuring mental disorder burden for action
articles.authors: Merete Nordentoft, Mette Falkenberg Krantz
articles.summary: Quantifying the burden of mental disorders is essential, yet inherently reductive. Metrics such as disability-adjusted life-years (DALYs) and years lived with disability (YLDs) provide a highly important common framework for comparing diseases across settings, but they only partly reflect lived experience. Such figures cannot fully capture the distress of a father whose daughter experiences persistent suicidal thoughts, or the impact on the quality of life of a child whose mother is unable to leave the house due to paranoid ideas.

articles.title: [Comment] Offline: The future of war
articles.authors: Richard Horton
articles.summary: Every week I have a 1-h call with a friend who lives on the US west coast, among tech entrepreneurs and fledgling billionaires. He is not a member of that tribe, but he does stalk their hinterland. Occasionally, he is invited to their top table, to contribute his own particular brand of esoteric wisdom, but more interestingly to eavesdrop on their thoughts and preoccupations. Perhaps because of where he lives, he leans towards techno-optimism. But sometimes optimism can trigger apocalyptic reflection.

articles.title: [World Report] Enhanced Games begin amid scientific concerns
articles.authors: Talha Burki
articles.summary: An athletics event at which participants are permitted to use performance-enhancing drugs has drawn criticism from sports scientists and anti-doping agencies. Talha Burki reports.

articles.title: [World Report] Are Trump's drug pricing policies saving anyone money?
articles.authors: Susan Jaffe
articles.summary: Experts question the effectiveness of the Trump administration's strategies to cut pharmaceutical costs in the USA. Washington Correspondent Susan Jaffe reports.

articles.title: [Perspectives] The age of ageing
articles.authors: Libby Sallnow, Richard Smith
articles.summary: What does ageing mean to us throughout our lives? How can we age better? These questions are central to The Coming of Age, a new Wellcome exhibition in London, UK. The show's title is taken from the English translation of Simone de Beauvoir's 1970 book La Vieillesse, the literal translation of which is “old age”. But, explains Lynne Segal, a professor emerita of gender and psychosocial studies, in the exhibition's accompanying book, “Such was the dread those two words provoked that when translated into English two years later publishers changed its title to The Coming of Age.” The exhibition comprises more than 120 objects and artworks and seeks to counter that dread by examining ageing across the life course, drawing on perspectives from different historical periods and global contexts.

articles.title: [Perspectives] Justice out of time: infected blood and patient knowledge
articles.authors: Fred Cooper
articles.summary: Questions over right and wrong in past medical practices all too frequently resist easy answers. Formal ethical frameworks, and the imperfect ways that they are interpreted and applied, are products of their time; and so are reflections on the past from the shifting and incomplete vantage point of the present. Historical injustices, such as the use of psychosurgery for behavioural and mental health problems during the mid-20th century, stretch understandings of what kinds of judgement are possible when a significant degree of time has elapsed.

articles.title: [Correspondence] The impact of conflict on patients with thalassaemia
articles.authors: Michael Angastiniotis, Panos Englezos, Androulla Eleftheriou
articles.summary: The Thalassaemia International Federation is issuing a belated, albeit necessary and desperate, plea on behalf of patients with thalassaemia who are direct witnesses and, increasingly, victims of armed conflict. Even sanctions, used as a non-military foreign policy tool and to gain the political upper hand, constitute a battlefield for these susceptible populations.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Loans, leases, and labour: the cost of becoming a doctor in the UK
articles.authors: Amy Still
articles.summary: 6 years ago, I questioned whether a medical career was financially realistic given my financial background and the funding options available to students. Now, as a fifth-year medical student at University College London (London, UK), that concern highlights a broader structural issue within UK medical education funding. In England, most medical students receive full tuition and maintenance support for the first 4 years. For those on 6-year programmes, the final 2 years are funded by a reduced maintenance loan, alongside a National Health Service bursary and variable supplementary support.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Effects of Venezuela's health system collapse on Latin America
articles.authors: Howard Lopes Ribeiro
articles.summary: The recent analysis by Sammer Marzouk and colleagues1 regarding Venezuela's collapsing health system highlights the urgent need to restore financing, infrastructure, and epidemiological surveillance amid political upheaval. Likewise, a Lancet Editorial2 emphasises that rebuilding public health functions and reliable data systems should be a priority for the country's recovery. These insights rightly focus on health system fragility, yet, an under-recognised threat is the destabilisation of scientific capacity in a country whose geographical, ecological, and economic assets are crucial to regional research and cooperation among low-income and middle-income countries.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Platelets must not be overlooked in civilian and military blood preparedness
articles.authors: Bingwen Eugene Fan
articles.summary: Nadira Frescaline and colleagues1 rightly emphasise whole blood and dried plasma as strategic priorities for civilian and military blood preparedness. However, their extension of planning for radiological incidents and nuclear explosions overlooks a crucial component: platelets. In such settings, fatal haemorrhage is not driven only by acute traumatic blood loss, but subsequently from bone marrow suppression causing severe thrombocytopenia. People who have had substantial radiation exposure can develop bone marrow failure with profound thrombocytopenia, and fatal haemorrhagic manifestations were well documented in numerous victims in the Hiroshima and Nagasaki atomic bombings, particularly 3–5 weeks post-exposure and were primary causes of death among radiation victims.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Carr-Hill formula recalibration to reflect rural disadvantage
articles.authors: Samuel P Trethewey, Christopher E Clark
articles.summary: Michael Anderson and colleagues1 highlight substantial challenges facing the UK Government's National Health Service (NHS) 10-year health plan for England. Shifting care from hospital to community, including with neighbourhood health centres, is a laudable goal but the plan fails to address implementation challenges facing rural health systems.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Challenging narratives on Syria's health system
articles.authors: Sophia Shalabi, Aula Abbara, Salah Safadi, Wassel Aljerk, Munzer Alkhalil
articles.summary: In their Correspondence, Bilal Irfan and colleagues highlight broad challenges facing Syria's health system.1 We emphasise the need for nuanced, evidence-driven analysis, grounded in the current reality of Syria's health system and embedded in establishing robust health system governance during this recovery phase.2 Some declarations made by Irfan and colleagues rely on data that are weakly evidenced or outdated in a rapidly evolving context. For example, although the effects of prolonged conflict and health system fragmentation on Syria's health-care workforce have been stark,3 the claim that 70% of health-care workers have been killed or forced to flee is widely questioned.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Challenging narratives on Syria's health system – Authors' reply
articles.authors: Bilal Irfan, M Ihsan Kaadan, Basel Tarab
articles.summary: We thank Sophia Shalabi and colleagues for engaging with our Correspondence.1 We agree that the recovery of the health system in Syria requires intentional and careful reforms to governance, oversight, regulatory structure, and protections for health-care workers, and careful dialogue between different stakeholders for an effective road to national recovery, rehabilitation, and trust building.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Outcome measure limitations in the SCIENCE trial
articles.authors: Amit Kalra, Harpreet Chhina, Anthony Cooper
articles.summary: The conclusions of the SCIENCE trial by Daniel C Perry and colleagues risk exceeding what the chosen primary outcome can legitimately support.1 The trial's primary endpoint—ie, the Patient-Reported Outcomes Measurement Information System (PROMIS) Pediatric Upper Extremity Computer Adaptive Test—contains no elbow-specific items and is dominated by activities of daily living. For displaced medial epicondyle fractures, the key clinical uncertainties that drive operative decision making are not “can the child button clothes or open a jar at 12 months?”,2 but valgus stability under load, pain or irritability with valgus stress, throwing and gymnastics performance or endurance, ulnar nerve symptoms, and confidence returning to high-demand sport.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Outcome measure limitations in the SCIENCE trial – Authors' reply
articles.authors: Daniel C Perry, Daphne Kounali, Alex Zimmermann, Juul Achten, Matthew L Costa
articles.summary: We thank Amit Kalra and colleagues for engaging with the SCIENCE study1 and welcome the opportunity to respond. We agree that an elbow-specific patient-reported outcome measure for children with medial epicondyle fractures would be valuable. However, no such instrument is currently established and validated for this indication. In the SCIENCE trial, the Patient Report Outcomes Measurement Information System (PROMIS) Upper Extremity Score for Children Computer Adaptive Test was prespecified in the protocol, supported by patient and public involvement, and chosen as a relevant measure of upper-limb function for this population.

articles.title: [Articles] Updated trends in the global prevalence and burden of mental disorders, 1990–2023: a systematic analysis for the Global Burden of Disease Study 2023
articles.authors: GBD 2023 Mental Disorder Collaborators
articles.summary: A significant health burden was imposed by mental disorders in all countries and territories in 2023, irrespective of the health resources available. In some instances, this burden has increased over time and is unevenly distributed across populations. Stronger surveillance systems, particularly in low-income and middle-income countries, are required. Additionally, we need more coordinated and inclusive policies to reduce the burden through early treatment and prevention, tailored to sex and age differences across locations.

articles.title: Salt-inducible kinases as druggable targets for immunotherapy of ovarian cancer
articles.summary: Nature Immunology, Published online: 21 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41590-026-02513-7Malignant ascites strongly inhibits T cell anti-tumor responses in patients with high-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSOC). Using an all-human high-throughput drug repurposing screen, we identified salt-inducible kinases (SIKs) as negative regulators of T cell function in ascites. Pharmacological inhibition or genetic depletion of SIK2 and SIK3 in T cells strongly upregulates anti-tumor immunity and improves survival in immunotherapy-resistant preclinical models.

articles.title: Mapping where mouse models match — and miss — human tumor immunity
articles.summary: Nature Immunology, Published online: 21 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41590-026-02527-1A systematic comparison of the tumor microenvironment (TME) in mouse models and humans reveals that widely used models capture only a limited subset of the cellular diversity and cell type-specific gene expression observed in human TMEs. We present a resource that defines the conserved and divergent features, including shared gene programs, across mouse and human TMEs to guide model selection and improve the translational reliability of immunotherapy studies.

articles.title: The host immune response to Mycobacterium tuberculosis determining protection or disease progression
articles.authors: Margarida Saraiva, William J. Branchett, Jyothi Rengarajan, Anne O’Garra
articles.summary: Nature Immunology, Published online: 18 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41590-026-02529-zO’Garra and colleagues discuss the evolving spectrum of M. tuberculosis infection outcomes and TB disease, how the host immune response determines and unfolds across this spectrum and how the natural diversity of M. tuberculosis contributes to this complexity.

articles.title: [Editorial] Psychedelics: after the renaissance
articles.authors: The Lancet
articles.summary: 20 years ago, The Lancet published an Editorial on reviving research into psychedelics for mental health conditions, observing that “the blanket ban on psychedelic drugs enforced in many countries continues to hinder safe and controlled investigation, in a medical environment, of their potential benefits”. The Lancet of 2006 could not have foreseen that one day a president of the USA would sign an executive order aiming to “dramatically accelerate access to new medical research and treatments based on psychedelic drugs”.

articles.title: [Comment] Emerging β-lactam and β-lactamase inhibitor strategies for complicated urinary tract infections
articles.authors: Marc Mendelson, Adrian Brink
articles.summary: Complicated urinary tract infections (cUTIs) and acute pyelonephritis remain major causes of hospitalisation worldwide and contribute substantially to antibiotic consumption and health-care costs.1 The increasing prevalence of antibiotic resistance among Gram-negative bacterial pathogens, particularly extended-spectrum β-lactamase (ESBL)-producing Enterobacterales and carbapenem-resistant bacteria has made effective treatment increasingly challenging.2

articles.title: [Comment] Vaccine impact in Gavi-supported countries: balancing evidence with policy needs
articles.authors: Isaac Osei, Baleng Mahama Wutor
articles.summary: Vaccines remain one of the most impactful and cost-effective public health interventions. Decades of evidence from randomised controlled trials and epidemiological studies have shown the impact of vaccines on population health following the introduction and scale-up of immunisation programmes.1 As the incidence of previously debilitating vaccine-preventable childhood diseases diminishes due to vaccination efforts, the perceived importance of immunisation could become less evident to the public. Amid rising vaccine hesitancy and the spread of misinformation, it is increasingly important to continue demonstrating the tangible impact of vaccines globally.

articles.title: [Comment] End of transplantation's reign in mantle cell lymphoma
articles.authors: Judith Trotman, Janlyn Falconer
articles.summary: Autologous stem-cell transplantation (ASCT) has remained central to the care of younger patients with mantle cell lymphoma since the publication of the first European Mantle Cell Lymphoma Network trial in 2005.1 Its role is now firmly challenged in The Lancet by results of the TRIANGLE study from Martin Dreyling and colleagues.2

articles.title: [Comment] Offline: Hantavirus—surprise, complacency, and peril
articles.authors: Richard Horton
articles.summary: Memories resurface. An unfamiliar virus. Deaths. A stricken cruise ship. Quarantines. Hastily arranged WHO press briefings. Scientists interviewed on radio and television about what is known—and, more importantly, not known—about disease transmission. WHO has assessed the public health risk as low. But for the families of the three passengers on the MV Hondius who died, together with those with either confirmed or suspected hantavirus infection, the news of an outbreak of a mysterious virus will be frightening.

articles.title: [World Report] Cleft surgery in Peru
articles.authors: Jacqui Thornton
articles.summary: Peru is expanding access to surgery and care for patients with cleft conditions in remote areas through intensive capacity-building led by local volunteers. Jacqui Thornton reports.

articles.title: [World Report] The medicine of…prisons
articles.authors: Talha Burki
articles.summary: As part of a series on medicine in unusual settings, Talha Burki takes a look at medical practice in UK prisons.

articles.title: [Perspectives] Thu-Anh Nguyen: building equitable access to health
articles.authors: Aarathi Prasad
articles.summary: “In Viet Nam, health challenges are rarely abstract”, says Thu-Anh Nguyen, Director of the University of Sydney Vietnam Institute (SVI) and Professor in Global Health at the University of Sydney, Australia. “They are visible in overcrowded clinics, in families navigating long treatment journeys, and in communities where preventable diseases persist. Building health research in this context is not simply about generating evidence. It is about creating the conditions under which evidence can be trusted, used, and sustained.”

articles.title: [Perspectives] Digitising the thymus
articles.authors: Hugo J W L Aerts, Eric J Topol
articles.summary: For decades, the thymus has been regarded as a vestigial organ of childhood, essential for T-cell education early in life, but thought to lose relevance as it involutes into fatty tissue after adolescence. This dogma was challenged a few years ago when a study showed that removal of the thymus during thoracic surgery was linked to poor outcomes. More recent work, enabled by artificial intelligence (AI), brings new insights about the potential role of the thymus across the lifespan.

articles.title: [Perspectives] Medicine, psychotherapy, and artificial intelligence
articles.authors: Caleb Gardner, Arthur Kleinman
articles.summary: The use of artificial intelligence (AI) and generative AI in medicine provokes important questions about the nature of clinical work performed by human practitioners as well as the limitations and benefits of this technology. In the light of the possibilities and pitfalls of AI in clinical work, there is a need to re-examine the basic nature of the therapeutic value of human interaction in clinical medicine and to discuss some pervasive misconceptions (in practice if not in theory) about the nature of the societally embedded human mind.

articles.title: [Obituary] Nancy J Cox
articles.authors: Faith McLellan
articles.summary: Virologist and expert in influenza surveillance, pandemic preparedness, and infectious disease response. Born on July 21, 1948, in Emmetsburg, IA, USA, she died from glioblastoma on April 24, 2026, in Atlanta, GA, aged 77 years.

articles.title: [Correspondence] FRESHAIR4Life: youth advocacy restoring trust in health institutions
articles.authors: Antonios Christodoulakis, Izolde Bouloukaki, Rianne M J J van der Kleij, Siân Williams, Ioanna Tsiligianni
articles.summary: We read with interest Marcello Ienca and colleagues’ Review on the increasing erosion of trust in health institutions.1 We also believe that restoring emotional engagement and resonance are essential, but were surprised that the authors did not suggest youth advocacy as a scalable strategy and co-production mechanism (WHO framework) for rebuilding trust, which encompasses most aspects of their proposed solutions.2

articles.title: [Correspondence] Conversational AI and the amplified trust paradox in health care
articles.authors: Carlos Fernando Mourão, Luiz Eduardo Juliasse, Bruno César Vasconcelos Gurgel
articles.summary: Marcello Ienca and colleagues1 in their Review identify a trust paradox in which rigorous institutions lose credibility while unaccountable voices gain it. We argue that large language model (LLM)-based chatbots represent a qualitatively distinct escalation of this paradox, extending beyond the social media dynamics described.

articles.title: [Correspondence] The plight of the Monaco microstate in the GBD 2023 study
articles.authors: Grant M A Wyper, Sarah Cuschieri, Brecht Devleesschauwer
articles.summary: The Global Burden of Diseases, Injuries, and Risk Factors Study (GBD) 2023, although invaluable for global health metrics, presents concerning estimates of life expectancy at birth for Monaco.1 Monaco is a microstate typically synonymous with world-leading levels of life expectancy at birth, with official sources, such as statistics from the Monaco Government and UN reports, consistently placing Monaco's life expectancy among the highest globally.2,3 The most recent estimate from the Monegasque Demography Observatory for 2022–24 places life expectancy at birth at 87·0 years;3 however, GBD estimates are substantially lower, at 80·4 years in 2023.

articles.title: [Correspondence] The plight of the Monaco microstate in the GBD 2023 study – Authors' reply
articles.authors: Simon I Hay, Catherine Bisignano, Christopher J L Murray
articles.summary: Grant M A Wyper and colleagues highlight a discrepancy between the Global Burden of Diseases, Injuries, and Risk Factors Study (GBD) 2023 life expectancy estimates for the microstate of Monaco and locally published figures. We welcome engagement on small-population estimation and offer several clarifications.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Proton versus photon therapy for oropharyngeal cancer
articles.authors: David J Sher
articles.summary: I congratulate Steven J Frank and colleagues1 on the successful completion of their multi-institutional study comparing photons with protons for oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, but many questions remain. One of the main differences between intensity-modulated (photon) radiation therapy (IMRT) and intensity-modulated proton therapy (IMPT) was the risk of grade 3 xerostomia, which is defined by Common Toxicity Criteria for Adverse Events (CTCAE) as dry mouth leading to the inability to adequately aliment orally, tube feeding or total parenteral nutrition indicated, or unstimulated salivary flow rate of less than 0·1 mL/min.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Proton versus photon therapy for oropharyngeal cancer
articles.authors: Sean M McBride, Nadeem Riaz, Eric J Sherman, C Jillian Tsai, Loren K Mell
articles.summary: Trials of local therapies where progression-free survival is equivalent but overall survival is improved raise the spectre of confounding. The overall survival improvement seen in the phase 3 trial by Steven J Frank and colleagues1 of proton therapy for oropharyngeal cancer is likely an example of this issue. First, if proton therapy truly increased survival—given the crossover from photon to proton and barring extreme bias in the crossover population—the overall survival difference should be attenuated in the intention-to-treat versus per-protocol comparison, which is not demonstrated.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Proton versus photon therapy for oropharyngeal cancer – Authors' reply
articles.authors: Steven J Frank, Paul M Busse, J Jack Lee, Robert L Foote, University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center Clinical Trial Consortium
articles.summary: We thank David J Sher for the compliments on our phase 3 trial of intensity-modulated (photon) radiation therapy (IMRT) versus intensity-modulated proton therapy (IMPT) for oropharyngeal cancer.1 A key finding from the trial was that proton therapy de-intensified chemoradiation therapy, reducing the rates of high-grade toxicities and gastrostomy tube dependency to 40·2% in the IMRT group and 26·8% in the IMPT group. Sher expressed concern that the reported rate of severe xerostomia (45% IMPT and 33% IMRT) was higher than that in the RTOG 1016 trial,2 where 243 (61·5%) of 395 patients given IMRT and cisplatin required a gastrotomy tube.

articles.title: [Articles] Efficacy and safety of cefepime–nacubactam and aztreonam–nacubactam compared with imipenem–cilastatin for complicated urinary tract infection or acute uncomplicated pyelonephritis (Integral-1): a double-blind, randomised phase 3 trial
articles.authors: Satoshi Takahashi, Kazuhiro Tateda, Katsunori Yanagihara, Haihui Huang, Yohei Doi, Mitsuru Yasuda, Kazuaki Matsumoto, Masayo Sumiya, Risako Takaya, Keisuke Suwada, Satoshi Kamiyabu, Yuji Sasagawa, Takeshi Minamida, Seiji Kato, Kenichiro Kondo, Takeshi Naruse, Hiroshige Mikamo
articles.summary: Cefepime–nacubactam and aztreonam–nacubactam are potential treatment options for Gram-negative cUTI and acute uncomplicated pyelonephritis, including infections caused by antimicrobial-resistant strains.

articles.title: [Articles] Quantifying relative health impact across Gavi, the Vaccine Alliance's portfolio in 117 countries at the subregional level: a modelling study
articles.authors: Katy A M Gaythorpe, Xiang Li, Manjari Shankar, Anna-Maria Hartner, Zoë Gibney, Kaja Abbas, Romesh Abeysuriya, Christina Alam, Megan Auzenbergs, Andrew S Azman, Edwine Barasa, Alan Costello, Matthew J Ferrari, Keith Fraser, Han Fu, Lydia Haile, Romain Glèlè Kakaï, Andromachi Karachaliou-Prasinou, Elizabeth C Lee, Esther Nyadzua Katama, Jong-Hoon Kim, Mark Jit, Yang Liu, Josephine Malinga, Sean Moore, Shevanthi Nayagam, Gemma Nedjati-Gilani, Lucy C Okell, Akindele Akano Onifade, Timos Papadopoulos, Melissa A Penny, T Alex Perkins, Virginia E Pitzer, Allison Portnoy, Simon R Procter, Chitra Maharani Saraswati, Nick Scott, Chris Seaman, Andrew J Shattock, So Yoon Sim, Quan Tran, Emilia Vynnycky, Amy K Winter, Wes Hinsley, Neil M Ferguson, Caroline L Trotter
articles.summary: Decisions around which vaccines to use are increasingly important in the context of Gavi's country vaccine budgets. Robust metrics that allow comparison between vaccines are thus essential to inform discussions. The vaccine impact ratios presented in this study can be used to complement other evidence to support effective planning and prioritisation in national immunisation programmes.

articles.title: [Comment] RTS,S/AS01 implementation reduces mortality in African children
articles.authors: Claudia A Daubenberger, Ally Olotu
articles.summary: WHO's approval of the RTS,S/AS01 (Mosquirix) malaria vaccine in 2021 marked a milestone in global public health. It represented not only the first vaccine approved against any parasitic human disease but also a concrete step towards reducing the incidence of clinical malaria and its associated mortality.

articles.title: [Comment] Could enpatoran add to our therapeutic toolbox in SLE?
articles.authors: Lia McCann, Laura Durcan
articles.summary: Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is a clinically and serologically heterogeneous condition in which therapeutic advances have been slower than for other rheumatic diseases. Only two new therapies have been licensed for use since the 1950s.1,2 These target B-cell activating factor (BAFF)1 and type-1 interferon receptors,2 but are often insufficient as monotherapy and are costly with varied global availability. Patient outcomes continue to be negatively affected by an over-reliance on corticosteroids and a heavy reliance on broad immunosuppression.

articles.title: [Comment] Fabricated references: a new threat to editorial integrity
articles.authors: Howard Bauchner, Frederick P Rivara
articles.summary: In this issue of The Lancet, Topaz and colleagues1 report the first systematic evaluation of the authenticity of references. The authors initially identified 125 615 773 references from 2 471 758 papers drawn from PubMed Central's Open Access platform published from Jan 1, 2023, to Feb 18, 2026. Of these references, only 77% (97·1 million) carried a PubMed ID and were further evaluated. References, after passing through several filters to account for spelling errors in names or incorrect titles, were then verified against PubMed, Crossref, OpenAlex, and Google Scholar.

articles.title: [Comment] Offline: Climate and health—time to step up our activism
articles.authors: Richard Horton
articles.summary: It is official: global warming is accelerating. In a paper published in Geophysical Research Letters in March, 2026, Grant Foster and Stefan Rahmstorf strip out the noise of natural variability in temperature measurements and conclude “with over 98% confidence” that planetary warming has speeded up since 2015. They call this acceleration “large and significant”. Why is it taking place? They speculate that reductions in air pollution might have diminished atmospheric cooling aerosols. Their conclusion is unusually blunt for a scientific paper: “Stopping this trend is in our hands: studies show that global warming will stop around the time humanity reaches zero CO2 emissions, but it can hardly be reversed.

articles.title: [World Report] Lebanon's health system: a silent casualty of war
articles.authors: Amelie David
articles.summary: Lebanon's already weakened health system is overwhelmed, under-resourced, and increasingly attacked by the Israeli army. Amelie David reports from Lebanon.

articles.title: [World Report] “We don’t want it to happen to others”: suicide in young Māori
articles.authors: Sophie Cousins, Clive Aspin, Suzy Taylor
articles.summary: Suicide is the leading cause of death in New Zealanders aged 15–19 years. Advocates are calling for better approaches to prevention and support. Sophie Cousins, Clive Aspin, and Suzy Taylor report.

articles.title: [Perspectives] When doctors do not care
articles.authors: Lucy Foulkes
articles.summary: There is a folder on my computer that I avoid as much as possible. It is called Health and it holds the clinical letters and other documents from all the consultants and other health-care professionals I have seen over the past 20 years. There are subfolders: gynaecology, urology, neurology, dermatology, dentists, physiotherapists, nutritionists. I brace myself whenever I need to go into that folder, because of everything it represents: 20 years of botched attempts to understand why my body was failing in so many different ways.

articles.title: [Obituary] J Michael Bishop
articles.authors: Faith McLellan
articles.summary: Physician–scientist, Nobel Laureate, Professor and Chancellor of the University of California, San Francisco. Born on Feb 22, 1936, in York, PA, USA, he died from pneumonia in San Francisco, CA, USA, on March 20, 2026, aged 90 years.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Fabricated citations: an audit across 2·5 million biomedical papers
articles.authors: Maxim Topaz, Nir Roguin, Pallavi Gupta, Zhihong Zhang, Laura-Maria Peltonen
articles.summary: Scientific literature depends on the integrity of its references. Each reference implicitly asserts that a verifiable source exists and supports the claims being made. When references point to non-existent studies, readers, reviewers, and policy makers are unable to evaluate the evidence.

articles.title: [Correspondence] The pharyngeal sanctuary: a challenge for zoliflodacin
articles.authors: Jian-Ying Wang
articles.summary: The phase 3 trial by Alison Luckey and colleagues1 validating zoliflodacin marks a pivotal moment in gonorrhoea management. As a novel agent targeting DNA GyrB subunit, zoliflodacin bypasses the GyrA-mediated resistance mechanisms that compromise fluoroquinolones, offering a viable option for use against multidrug-resistant strains.1,2

articles.title: [Correspondence] Zoliflodacin: non-inferior, but not equivalent?
articles.authors: Anselm Jorda, Markus Zeitlinger
articles.summary: We congratulate Alison Luckey and colleagues 1 of the phase 3 zoliflodacin trial on the successful evaluation of a novel oral antimicrobial agent against uncomplicated gonorrhoea. Novel agents with distinct pharmacodynamic targets, such as GyrB inhibition by zoliflodacin, are urgently needed.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Zoliflodacin: non-inferior, but not equivalent? – Authors’ reply
articles.authors: Alison Luckey, Manica Balasegaram, Drew Lewis, Nicole Close, Edward W Hook
articles.summary: We thank Anselm Jorda and Markus Zeitlinger for highlighting the important distinction between statistical non-inferiority, inferiority, and equivalence.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Toxicology-informed sequencing to optimise cancer therapy
articles.authors: Gang Lv, Jing Yuan, Xiangxiang Jiang, Jun Wu, Z Kevin Lu
articles.summary: We commend David J Lewis and colleagues1 for demonstrating that ibrutinib–rituximab can reach chemotherapy-free disease control in older patients with mantle-cell lymphoma who were not eligible for transplantation. The ENRICH trial represents a pivotal advance, yet its findings invite refinement for broader clinical practice. First, the absence of minimal residual disease (MRD) monitoring reduces the opportunity to identify patients who could safely discontinue Bruton tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibition once deep molecular remission is reached.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Toxicology-informed sequencing to optimise cancer therapy - Authors’ reply
articles.authors: David J Lewis, Simon Rule
articles.summary: We thank Gang Lv and colleagues for the response to our study.1 We agree that minimal residual disease (MRD) monitoring could help to optimise the treatment of mantle-cell lymphoma with ibrutinib and other Bruton tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors. However, no study comparing outcomes of continuous use of BTK inhibitors versus an MRD-driven approach has yet been reported. Currently, continuous therapy with BTK inhibitors remains the standard of care in most first-line mantle-cell lymphoma studies.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Climate adaptation must address global inequity of suffering
articles.authors: Cara Platts, Jennifer Philip, Paul Komesaroff
articles.summary: The 2025 Lancet Countdown on health and climate change highlights the accelerating human toll of climate inaction and documents record heat-related deaths, widespread food insecurity, and intensifying climate-driven disease risks.1 However, one aspect of the health response that is becoming increasingly urgent as the climate emergency intensifies remains absent from the report: equitable access to palliative and end-of-life care.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Broadening metrics in the Lancet Countdown on health and climate change
articles.authors: Chris Newman
articles.summary: Reading the Lancet Countdown,1 I felt both concern and unease. The evidence of worsening climate-related health impacts is deeply saddening. But the Countdown's treatment of energy felt narrow, presenting renewables as the only solution while omitting other low-carbon options. Having been a committed climate activist, I have since spent several years trying to understand why energy debates have become polarised. One recurring concern is the narrative that fossil fuels are bad and renewables are good, although directionally correct, this oversimplifies system-level challenges around cost and reliability.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Broadening metrics in the Lancet Countdown on health and climate change
articles.authors: Alexander Salazar-Ceballos, Doriam Camacho-Rodríguez, Lídice Alvarez-Miño
articles.summary: We read with interest the recent Lancet Countdown report,1 which is another advance in the scientific evidence of the impact of climate change on human health.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Broadening metrics in the Lancet Countdown on health and climate change – Authors’ reply
articles.authors: Marina Romanello, Ian Hamilton, Shih-Che Hsu
articles.summary: We thank Chris Newman for the thoughtful input. We share the interest in ensuring that the Lancet Countdown on health and climate change is tracking actions that support rapid greenhouse gas emissions reduction aligned with health protection and promotion.

articles.title: [Articles] Impact of introducing RTS,S/AS01E malaria vaccine on mortality in young children in Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi: an observational evaluation of a cluster-randomised implementation programme
articles.authors: Victor Mwapasa, Kwaku Poku Asante, Paul Milligan, Samuel Akech, Abraham Oduro, Don P Mathanga, Ari Fogelson, Titus K Kwambai, Mary J Hamel, Atupele Kapito-Tembo, Thomas Gyan, Nelli Westercamp, Rafiq N A Okine, Kerryn A Moore, Eliane Pellaux-Furrer, Christopher C Stanley, Daniel Ansong, Simon Kariuki, Patricia Njuguna, Tisungane Mvalo, Paul Welaga, Lucas Otieno, Paul Snell, David Schellenberg, Alfred Chimala, Edwin A Afari, Philip Bejon, Kenneth Maleta, Tsiri Agbenyega, Robert W Snow, Madalitso Zulu, Eliezer Odei-Lartey, Jobiba Chinkhumba, Aaron M Samuels, Malaria Vaccine Programme Evaluation partners
articles.summary: Introduction of the RTS,S malaria vaccine in routine immunisation programmes was associated with a significant reduction in mortality in young children, averting about one in eight deaths, in areas with moderate coverage of three doses of the vaccine and low uptake of the fourth dose. These results highlight the urgency to accelerate the deployment of malaria vaccines in areas of Africa where malaria continues to be a leading cause of child mortality.

articles.authors: The Lancet
articles.summary: Legislation imposing a ban on anyone in the UK born after 2008 from buying cigarettes and other tobacco products was always likely to be contentious. Predictably, The UK Tobacco and Vapes Bill—shortly due to be signed into law by King Charles III—has provoked much debate; industry has pushed back against the law claiming the ban was unnecessary and that it violated trade agreements. Politicians seeking election have vowed to overturn the sales ban at the earliest opportunity. But the Bill has the potential to create huge gains in public health for future generations by precisely targeting the very point at which addiction to nicotine usually starts—during the teenage years.

articles.title: [Comment] GLP-1 therapies: an emerging approach for alcohol reduction?
articles.authors: Christian S Hendershot, Klara R Klein
articles.summary: Alcohol use disorder is among the most prevalent and under-treated health conditions worldwide.1 Despite its high global disease burden and association with myriad comorbidities, including cardiometabolic disease and alcohol-related liver disease, alcohol use disorder maintains one of the largest treatment gaps of any health condition.1,2 Few alcohol use disorder medications have seen regulatory approval. These medications are sorely underused in high-income countries (<2% of those with alcohol use disorder in the USA2) and often inaccessible in middle-income and low-income countries.

articles.title: [Comment] HPV vaccine scale-up is key to curb rising cervical cancer inequalities
articles.authors: Gary M Clifford
articles.summary: In 2026, cervical cancer remains a stark reminder of global health inequity, with women in low-income countries and lower-middle-income countries (LMICs) with a disproportionate share of the disease burden. Although prevalence of human papillomavirus (HPV)—the necessary cause of cervical cancer—varies among populations, the dominant driver of global disparities is unequal access to effective cervical screening. High-income countries (HICs) that implemented cytology-based screening programmes some 50 years ago, and later adopted HPV-based programmes, have prevented HPV-driven cervical cancer epidemics,1,2 which are seen today in many LMICs where only a minority of women have ever received a cervical screening examination.

articles.title: [Comment] Offline: Ten lessons for women's and children's health
articles.authors: Richard Horton
articles.summary: It began with fury. Jennifer Bryce wanted to know what The Lancet was doing about 10 million under-5 deaths taking place each year, most of which were preventable. We were not doing anything. And nor were the UN agencies, including UNICEF and WHO. Jennifer's fury was entirely justified. And so was born our first global health Series on child survival, published in 2003. Lesson 1: Never lose the capacity to be shocked into action by injustice and failure. That Series led to more: on newborn survival, maternal health, and sexual and reproductive health and rights.

articles.title: [World Report] Health on the ballot in Senedd Cymru election
articles.authors: Chloe Wilson
articles.summary: May 7 elections will likely see political change in Wales. With long waiting lists and concerns over staffing, what are the major parties promising to do? Chloe Wilson reports.

articles.title: [Perspectives] Amita Aggarwal: understanding autoimmune rheumatic diseases
articles.authors: Aarathi Prasad
articles.summary: As a child, Amita Aggarwal, clinical immunologist and rheumatologist, and the Executive Director of the All India Institute of Medical Sciences (AIIMS) Bibinagar, India, recalls living in many places. “My father worked for the Government of India, and every few years he would be posted to another place”, she says. “We were in Nepal for 5 years. In the 1971 India–Pakistan war, we were posted near the border; he was also posted to Iraq for some time.” These events gave Aggarwal new perspectives, and inspired in her a commitment to serve local communities.

articles.title: [Perspectives] Of memory, love, death, and Proust
articles.authors: Seamus O'Mahony
articles.summary: Being a neat, fastidious writer, it is fitting that Julian Barnes chose to publish his fifteenth and final novel Departure(s) on his 80th birthday. When asked by the BBC Radio 4 presenter John Wilson why he had decided to call it a day, Barnes replied: “Can you cite a good book written by someone after the age of eighty?”

articles.title: [Perspectives] Myopic medical harm: a man receives free colon cancer screening in Ghana
articles.authors: Joy E Obayemi, Kwabena Agbedinu, Gifty Kwakye
articles.summary: Kwame, a 60-year-old man living 30 miles from Kumasi in Ghana, worked as a farmer and financially supported his wife, three children, and large extended family. He had always been physically fit and spent most of his time working on his farm. When a friend told him about a free screening campaign organised by an international non-governmental organisation (NGO) as part of colorectal cancer awareness month, he eagerly took advantage of this free health care, given his limited access to health services.

articles.title: [Obituary] Robert Goodman
articles.authors: Andrew Green
articles.summary: Child psychiatrist, who developed two important child psychiatric assessment tools. Born on Nov 15, 1953, in London, UK, he died on Dec 18, 2025, from complex dementia in London, UK, aged 72 years.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Will the USA lose its measles elimination status?
articles.authors: Anne C Bischops, Erin N Hulland, Mia Patzakis, Maimuna S Majumder
articles.summary: The USA currently faces its largest measles outbreak in decades, with 2280 confirmed cases in 2025 and 910 additional cases reported in just the first 6 weeks of 2026.1 This re-emergence threatens one of the country's major public health achievements: the elimination of measles in 2000, a feat reached after several years of extensive nationwide vaccination efforts, including a change to a two-dose measles, mumps, and rubella vaccination schedule.1 The current epidemic started in January, 2025, in Texas, USA, with two imported cases, and has since spread to 45 US states.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Pastoral care and unmet need in acute mental health services
articles.authors: Michael Hilton, David Mitchell
articles.summary: Ministers of religion are often approached as a last resort when all else fails. When family and friends are in despair, when services are unresponsive, and when risk is escalating, it is frequently the priest, imam, or rabbi who is called. We are two rabbis experienced in care in faith communities, and we have learned what acute mental illness means for us: sitting with congregants, friends, and families at the darkest times of their lives, offering spiritual guidance and practical help.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Ultra-processed food policy must regulate the screen as well as the street
articles.authors: Yihan Hu
articles.summary: Gyorgy Scrinis and colleagues make a strong case for moving ultra-processed food (UPF) policy beyond narrow reformulation agendas towards broader fiscal, labelling, marketing, and retail measures.1 Yet one policy domain remains underdeveloped in this discussion: the digital food environment.

articles.title: [Correspondence] WHO global rehabilitation indicators meet rising health needs
articles.authors: Wouter de Groote, Carlotte Kiekens, Stefano Negrini, Guy Fones
articles.summary: At its 158th Executive Board, the Director-General of WHO presented a report outlining global indicators to monitor the integration of rehabilitation into health systems.1 The indicators were selected following a member states consultation between November, 2024, and March, 2025. This milestone follows the 2023 World Health Assembly (WHA) 76.6 Resolution for strengthening rehabilitation in health systems, which calls for developing rehabilitation services at all levels of the health system to address huge unmet population needs.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Decentralisation must be the future of global health
articles.authors: Josh Parker Allen, Stephen Asiimwe
articles.summary: The Offline piece1 by Richard Horton is right that global health is on the brink: funding is collapsing, political volatility persists unabated, and powerful actors are renegotiating international engagement. But the answer is not simply a new narrative organised around climate, pandemics, conflict, migration, demography, or digital transformation. What global health requires is structural reorientation.

articles.title: [Correspondence] The need for a new global health narrative
articles.authors: Steven L B Jensen
articles.summary: Richard Horton calls for a new narrative for global health to replace the narrative from the turn of the century that “rooted in macroeconomics” turned attention to the strategic importance of global health.1 Horton summarises the older narrative as follows: “extreme poverty is the biggest obstacle to sustainable economic growth; the causes of extreme poverty are a small number of preventable and treatable diseases; by tackling those diseases, poverty can be defeated and economic growth secured”.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Clinical implications and biomarkers in the PACT-21 CASSANDRA trial
articles.authors: John P Neoptolemos, Christoph Springfeld, Daniel Öhlund, Pascal Hammel, Daniel H Palmer
articles.summary: The PACT-21 CASSANDRA trial,1 reported by Michele Reni and colleagues, involving 260 patients with mixed resectable and borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC), concluded that “preoperative PAXG [cisplatin, nab-paclitaxel, capecitabine, and gemcitabine] should be considered as the standard comparator group for future trials in this setting”. However, the conclusion cannot be supported as the control arm used was not the standard of care, and mixing patients with mixed resectable and borderline resectable cancers prohibits accurate comparison with other trials since these have distinct survival pattens.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Clinical implications and biomarkers in the PACT-21 CASSANDRA trial
articles.authors: Christos Fountzilas, Han Yu, Zachary Stiles
articles.summary: We read with great interest the initial report on the PACT-21 CASSANDRA study by Michele Reni and colleagues.1 We argue that although the event-free survival benefit of PAXG (cisplatin, nab-paclitaxel, capecitabine, and gemcitabine) over mFOLFIRINOX (modified fluorouracil, leucovorin, irinotecan, and oxaliplatin) is statistically significant, the results are not practice-changing. The event-free survival benefit appears to be driven by the CA19-9 events. 21 patients in the mFOLFIRINOX group versus 12 patients in the PAXG group had a CA19-9 event; both numbers are above the fragility index2 of 5 and CA19-9 does not always correlate with radiological response.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Clinical implications and biomarkers in the PACT-21 CASSANDRA trial – Authors' reply
articles.authors: Michele Reni, Giulia Orsi, Michele Milella, Catia Carconi, Massimo Falconi
articles.summary: We thank John P Neoptolemos and colleagues and Christos Fountzilas and colleagues for sharing their opinions on the PACT-21 CASSANDRA study.1 Scarcity of phase 3 trials in pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) simplifies drawing evidence-based conclusions. We acknowledge that the adjuvant scenario in the highly selected population of resected patients has solidly established standards. However, these hold negligible relevance and are inapplicable to patients with a worse prognosis who are considered for neoadjuvant therapy.

articles.title: [Articles] Once-weekly semaglutide versus placebo in patients with alcohol use disorder and comorbid obesity: a randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial
articles.authors: Mette Kruse Klausen, Signe Keller Justesen, Julie Niemann Pedersen, Line Rasmussen, Andreas Jensen, Mathias Ebbesen Jensen, Ulla B Knorr, Marianne Lerbæk Bergmann, Jens Juul Holst, Bolette Hartmann, George F Koob, Helene Benveniste, Nora D Volkow, Claus Thorn Ekstrøm, Gitte Moos Knudsen, Tina Vilsbøll, Anders Fink-Jensen
articles.summary: Semaglutide showed robust therapeutic effects in treatment-seeking participants with obesity and alcohol use disorder and this trial supports previous preclinical and clinical findings suggesting GLP-1 receptor agonists as a potential novel treatment target for alcohol use disorder.

articles.authors: Early Breast Cancer Trialists' Collaborative Group (EBCTCG)
articles.summary: For premenopausal women with ER-positive early breast cancer, even if chemotherapy or tamoxifen are given, OFS significantly reduces the 15-year risk of recurrence and death.
