articles.title: [Editorial] The US CDC on the brink
articles.authors: The Lancet
articles.summary: On Aug 11, 2025, a gunman who falsely believed that mRNA COVID-19 vaccines were dangerous went on a shooting rampage at the US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention's (CDC) main campus, shattering 150 windows and tragically killing a 33-year-old CDC security officer. Staff, still reeling from firings and programmatic shifts that began with the new Trump administration, were deeply traumatised. 8 months later, the windows have not been repaired. The CDC was once the gold standard for public health leadership.

articles.title: [Comment] Antibody-based malaria prevention in an intense perennial transmission setting
articles.authors: Christian R Engwerda, Ashraful Haque
articles.summary: Despite substantial progress in malaria control over the past two decades, malaria caused by Plasmodium falciparum remains a leading cause of morbidity and mortality in sub-Saharan Africa, particularly in young children.1 Achievement of malaria elimination will require complementary interventions that target different stages of the parasite lifecycle and can be implemented in different transmission settings.2 In addition to vector control, chemoprevention, and vaccination, monoclonal antibodies targeting parasite antigens have emerged as a promising strategy for malaria prevention.

articles.title: [Comment] Cardiovascular benefit of blood-pressure lowering in chronic kidney disease
articles.authors: Yingxian Sun, Xiaofan Guo
articles.summary: Chronic kidney disease (CKD) has long remained at the margins of the evidence base for the cardiovascular benefits of blood-pressure lowering. The absolute risk of cardiovascular events in people with CKD is among the highest across major clinical populations.1 However, research into the cardiovascular benefits of blood-pressure lowering in this population has, for decades, relied largely on extrapolation, subgroup analyses, and secondary meta-analyses,2,3 rather than trials spanning multiple CKD stages with cardiovascular outcomes as primary endpoints.

articles.title: [Comment] Offline: President Trump—it is not too late
articles.authors: Richard Horton
articles.summary: A global tragedy is unfolding. Withdrawal of the US Government from the fight against HIV/AIDS is causing thousands of preventable infections and deaths worldwide. Over many decades, generous investments by the American people through the US Agency for International Development (USAID), the National Institutes of Health (NIH), and the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) brought hope and opportunity to communities affected by HIV. While multiple mathematical predictions of disaster have been published during the past year, few studies have examined the actual human consequences of America's retreat from global health.

articles.title: [World Report] New Government aims to revitalise health in Hungary
articles.authors: Ed Holt
articles.summary: The Tisza Party promises to increase investment, rejuvenate public health, and address staff dissatisfaction. Ed Holt reports.

articles.title: [World Report] Europe's medical AI reforms
articles.authors: Paul Webster
articles.summary: Experts have criticised the EU's plans to revise medical data governance, following concerted pressure from US tech companies. Paul Webster reports.

articles.title: [World Report] Outrage over UN–Nestlé partnership
articles.authors: Udani Samarasekera
articles.summary: An open letter calls for an end to a recent agreement with the UN University, based on Nestlé's history of promoting infant formula. Udani Samarasekera reports.

articles.title: [Perspectives] Amadou Sall: virologist committed to vaccine equity
articles.authors: Faith McLellan
articles.summary: Amadou Alpha Sall is a Senegalese virologist who worked for three decades at the Institut Pasteur de Dakar (IPD) before joining the Coalition for Epidemic Preparedness Innovations (CEPI), in 2025, as Executive Director for Manufacturing and Supply Chain. At CEPI he is focused on strategies to expand regional vaccine manufacturing and supply chains to increase pandemic preparedness.

articles.title: [Perspectives] Caring for the caregivers
articles.authors: Jess Libow
articles.summary: Laura Mauldin's latest book begins with a story of falling in love. “I was the one”, she recalls of the start of her relationship with “J”. When J is diagnosed with leukaemia, Mauldin becomes not only her “life partner” but also her sole “life-support system” until J's death nearly 5 years later. Throughout In Sickness and in Health: Love Stories from the Front Lines of America's Caregiving Crisis, Mauldin weaves her and J's story together with those of other couples navigating illness and disability.

articles.title: [Perspectives] How does it feel to live with kidney disease?
articles.authors: Roland Bleiker, Zoltan Endre, Sophie Harman
articles.summary: Kidney disease is a major global health issue with implications for the lives of millions of people and for the cost of health care. Yet there is surprisingly little public awareness about the symptoms of kidney disease and what it means to live on dialysis or with a transplant. In addition, medical practice and research focus primarily, and understandably, on clinical aspects, rather than the lived experience of patients.

articles.title: [Obituary] Robin Weiss
articles.authors: Talha Burki
articles.summary: Influential pioneer of viral oncology who advanced knowledge of HIV/AIDS and cancer biology. Born on Feb 20, 1940, in London, UK, he died on Feb 27, 2026, in London, UK, aged 86 years

articles.title: [Correspondence] Systematic health destruction in Iran by Operation Epic Fury
articles.authors: Iradj Sobhani, Ali Keshavarzian, Asghar Rastegar, Mohsen Shahmanesh
articles.summary: The military campaign initiated on Feb 28, 2026, by the USA and Israel against Iran has rapidly evolved into a regional conflagration with catastrophic health consequences for Iran as well as for the region. The sheer ferocity and duration of the strikes of this so-called preventive action to dismantle nuclear and missile capabilities, intentionally or not, has led to violation of International Humanitarian Law (IHL) and the foreseeable collapse of civilian life-sustaining systems for Iranian citizens, including those who have suffered gravely under Islamic Republic oppressive rules and brutal crackdown.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Microbiological governance and the Lancet Commission on Ukraine
articles.authors: Guido Granata, Clive Kilgallen, Nicola Petrosillo
articles.summary: We read with interest the Commission's Comment by Adrianna Murphy and colleagues, outlining a roadmap for the Ukrainian health system.1 A key issue requires urgent integration into the strategic framework—the emergence and dissemination of multidrug-resistant (MDR) bacteria owing to the ongoing war.2 The implications of such MDR bacteria spread are far-reaching. MDR bacteria outbreaks burden fragile health systems that are already struggling with reconstruction. Escalating antimicrobial resistance (AMR) could result in increased mortality from sepsis, prolonged hospital stays, and health-care expenditure.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Microbiological governance and the Lancet Commission on Ukraine – Authors' reply
articles.authors: Adrianna Murphy, Nataliia Riabtseva, Pavlo Kovtoniuk, Erica Richardson
articles.summary: We welcome and appreciate the Correspondence from Guido Granata and colleagues. We agree that antimicrobial resistance (AMR) poses an existential global threat and has substantial implications for the sustainability of all health systems. Conflict raises the risk of inpatient AMR transmission, but we propose that strengthening infection prevention and control and reducing the burden of hospital-acquired infections are essential for improving quality of care and patient safety, whether AMR is an explicit concern or not.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Non-coeliac gluten sensitivity: rethinking challenge studies
articles.authors: Armin Alaedini, Jimin Yang, Benjamin Lebwohl, Peter H Green
articles.summary: Jessica R Biesiekierski and colleagues1 provide a timely and comprehensive review of non-coeliac gluten sensitivity (NCGS), emphasising substantial heterogeneity in diagnostic approaches, challenge protocols, and symptom assessment. The authors conclude that these methodological weaknesses have impeded progress towards identifying the molecular triggers of this condition. These issues were systematically identified in our scoping analysis of double-blind, placebo-controlled challenge trials, which documented the extent of methodological heterogeneity in this literature and highlighted several recurrent weaknesses.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Non-coeliac gluten sensitivity: rethinking challenge studies – Author's reply
articles.authors: Jessica R Biesiekierski
articles.summary: I thank Armin Alaedini and colleagues for their Correspondence and for reinforcing the need for methodological rigour in challenge studies of non-coeliac gluten sensitivity (NCGS).

articles.title: [Correspondence] Off-target benefits of influenza vaccination in cardiovascular disease
articles.authors: Ole Fröbert, Ida B Pedersen, Astrid J Hjelholt, Christian Erikstrup, Sara Cajander
articles.summary: We read with interest Craig S Anderson and colleagues’ PANDA II cluster-randomised trial of influenza vaccination in heart failure.1 Vaccination during admission improved survival and reduced readmissions at 12 months, with a number needed to vaccinate of 27. This is a rigorous and clinically important study. However, potential off-target effects of vaccination were not considered in the design or interpretation.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Off-target benefits of influenza vaccination in cardiovascular disease – Authors' reply
articles.authors: Xin Du, Craig S Anderson
articles.summary: We thank Ole Fröbert and colleagues for their interest in our study and proposing that vaccination might protect against cardiovascular disease through mechanisms beyond infection prevention, potentially through immunomodulatory effects. We agree this is an interesting and important hypothesis.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Semaglutide, weight loss, and cardiovascular outcomes in the SELECT trial
articles.authors: Justin Tondt, Vernon M Chinchilli
articles.summary: John Deanfield and colleagues reported that semaglutide reduces major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE) independent of weight loss.1 However, this conclusion relies on conditioning on a post-randomisation mediator (weight loss), which is a method known to potentially introduce collider stratification bias.2

articles.title: [Correspondence] Semaglutide, weight loss, and cardiovascular outcomes in the SELECT trial
articles.authors: Sridhar Mangalesh, Panagiotis Theodoropoulos, Kuan-Yu Chi, Michael G Nanna
articles.summary: In prespecified analyses from the SELECT trial, semaglutide reduced major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE) compared with placebo, with only about one-third of benefit mediated by waist circumference change.1 John Deanfield and colleagues report multiple interesting findings in the placebo group. Baseline bodyweight was unrelated to MACE, whereas smaller baseline waist circumference predicted lower risk in this group. Early percentage weight change showed no linear benefit, but a significant quadratic pattern was seen, with excess events among those losing 5% or more of their bodyweight; these participants also had disproportionately higher non-cardiovascular mortality.

articles.title: Developing a vaccine strategy to prevent the progression of pulmonary fibrosis
articles.authors: R. Gisli Jenkins
articles.summary: Nature Immunology, Published online: 20 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41590-026-02487-6Pulmonary fibrosis is an incurable chronic, progressive disease characterized by the emergence of fibrosis-specific cells, including a subset of fibroblasts. Proof-of-concept experiments in mice now show that the immune system can be mobilized by vaccination to target these cells and limit the development of fibrosis.

articles.title: [Editorial] Abortion: the possibilities of progress
articles.authors: The Lancet
articles.summary: Women's bodily autonomy and health, particularly with regard to abortion, are under attack. The politicisation of women's bodies and choices is part of a wider attempt to roll back human rights and freedoms of women and marginalised groups. Political parties with regressive ideologies, rising across the world, are finding common cause with anti-gender religious groups. Transnational anti-gender movements have become professionalised and influence national and international agendas. Overseas aid has become a bargaining chip for abortion and gender rights, with dire consequences to sexual and reproductive health.

articles.title: [Comment] Offline: Reinvigorating One Health—merci!
articles.authors: Richard Horton
articles.summary: One lament you will often hear from global health activists is the lack of commitment, even interest, shown by high-income country political leaders towards health and health equity. There have been exceptions. Norway's Jens Stoltenberg (vaccination). Canada's Stephen Harper (women's and children's health). America's Bill Clinton (HIV). Still, indifference is commonly the default. Last week, France's President, Emmanuel Macron, defied the trend by hosting an international Summit on One Health in Lyon.

articles.title: [World Report] Lyon commitments aim to advance One Health
articles.authors: Faith McLellan
articles.summary: French President Emmanuel Macron promises action on One Health, with a suite of pledges announced at the ninth One Health Summit. Faith McLellan reports from Lyon.

articles.title: [World Report] Disasters in Vanuatu: keeping mortality low
articles.authors: Chris McCall
articles.summary: Vanuatu has a long history of natural disasters. But a combination of traditional knowledge and a simple warning system have kept deaths remarkably low. Chris McCall reports.

articles.title: [World Report] The medicine of…nuclear power plants
articles.authors: Talha Burki
articles.summary: As part of a continuing series, Talha Burki looks at the issues at the core of medicine in nuclear power plants.

articles.title: [Perspectives] Refiloe Masekela: building access to care for childhood lung disease
articles.authors: Aarathi Prasad
articles.summary: “In the 2000s, when the AIDS pandemic hit South Africa, we were seeing a lot of children dying from HIV. That was just before antiretroviral treatment had become fully accessible for people in the state sector”, recalls Refiloe Masekela, a paediatric pulmonologist who is Dean of the School of Medicine at the University of KwaZulu Natal and a faculty member at the Africa Health Research Institute (AHRI) in Durban, South Africa. At the time, she was still completing her paediatric training at the University of Pretoria.

articles.title: [Perspectives] On a heating planet, do humans and corals face a shared risk?
articles.authors: David S Jones
articles.summary: Corals have long captured the medical imagination. As Earth heats up, we might want to take our linked fates more seriously. It is easy to see how rising temperatures threaten humans and corals alike. Researchers initially dismissed the idea that carbon dioxide on its own could threaten our health. Now it seems that carbon dioxide disrupts the physiologies of corals and humans in ways we do not yet fully understand.

articles.title: [Perspectives] Demon Copperhead and the reach of social realism
articles.authors: Daniel Marchalik, Ethan Booker
articles.summary: In a 1963 LIFE magazine interview, writer James Baldwin captured why books make us feel less alone: “You think your pain and your heartbreak are unprecedented in the history of the world”, he reflected, “but then you read. It was Dostoevsky and Dickens who taught me that the things that tormented me most were the very things that connected me with all the people who were alive, or who ever had been alive.” These writers not only revealed the universality of human experience but also gave voice to lives that were often overlooked.

articles.title: [Obituary] David George Legge
articles.authors: Andrew Green
articles.summary: Public health academic, global health visionary, and health activist who co-founded the People's Health Movement. Born on April 22, 1944, in Sydney, NSW, Australia, he was diagnosed with terminal cancer and took his leave with voluntary assisted dying on Feb 2, 2026, in Melbourne, VIC, Australia, aged 81 years.

articles.title: [Correspondence] The permission gap and moral velocity
articles.authors: Alaa Murabit
articles.summary: When I started practising medicine in Libya, I learned something we do not teach in medical school: health access and interventions mean nothing if the community has not authorised a woman to use them. That is what drove me to create Voice of Libyan Women and build a faith-aligned movement.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Structural erosion of epidemiology and Japan's next generation
articles.authors: Tomoki Kawahara, Mako Nagayoshi, Yuto Maeda, Yu Par Khin, Bomi Park
articles.summary: Japan's scientific standing has faced a quiet crisis over the past two decades. Although it maintained high absolute research and development spending, Japan's global share of the top 10% of highly cited papers plummeted from fourth in the early 2000s to 12th in 2023.1 This decline is a symptom of long-lasting selection and concentration policies that threaten both the sustainability of epidemiological research and the scientific workforce.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Parkinson's disease: extending collaboration to Latin America
articles.authors: Vitor M Arca, Jacy B Parmera, Artur F S Schuh, Pedro R P Brandão, Henrique B Ferraz
articles.summary: We read with enthusiasm the Comment1 by Andrew Singleton and colleagues about the role of international collaboration in advancing understanding of Parkinson's disease biology. The identification of GBA1 as a major risk factor for Parkinson's disease in west African populations is a clear example of global collaborative efforts.2 Enabled by initiatives such as the Global Parkinson's Genetics Program, the work did more than verify known mechanisms; it showed how much relevant biology remains unrecognised when large regions of the world are excluded from research efforts.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Doorstep delivery outpaces continuity: Tamil Nadu's NCD care
articles.authors: Jeevarathinam Thirumalai, Indra Sivakumar, Saravanan Sekaran
articles.summary: We read with interest the World Report by Dinesh C Sharma on Makkalai Thedi Maruthuvam (MTM, which translates as Reaching out Health Services to People), describing the expansion of doorstep delivery for non-communicable disease (NCD) care in Tamil Nadu, India, through population screening and medicine provision.1 MTM represents an important advance in reducing access barriers, particularly for individuals with low mobility or socioeconomic constraints. However, MTM also raises a broader systems question: whether access-focused delivery innovations can improve chronic disease outcomes without parallel investments in continuity of care.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Rocatinlimab atopic dermatitis in two global phase 3 clinical trials
articles.authors: Khushi Gupta, Michael Ghebrial, Chiara Tognaccini, Dina Ghanim, Jashin J Wu
articles.summary: The study1 by Emma Guttman-Yassky and colleagues represents an important and innovative contribution to the evolving treatment landscape of moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis, particularly by targeting OX40. Use of a placebo and allowance of rescue therapy, even for patients who did not respond to systemic treatments, is clinically relevant. However, classifying all patients needing rescue therapy as permanent non-responders complicates efficacy interpretation. The sensitivity analysis, which included patients with less than 2 weeks of consecutive rescue therapy, attempted to address this issue, but confounding from the type, start date, and total exposure of rescue therapy remains likely.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Scheduled birth at term: benefit or trade-off?
articles.authors: David Baud, Joanna Sichitiu, Hélène Legardeur, Pauline de Vries, Manon Vouga
articles.summary: James Goadsby and colleagues report that risk-stratified scheduled birth at term reduces the incidence of pre-eclampsia without increasing emergency caesarean section or neonatal morbidity.1 Although the trial is carefully conducted, several contextual aspects limit its applicability and weaken conclusions as to whether routine management, including post-dates delivery, remains a safe option.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Scheduled birth at term: benefit or trade-off? – Authors' reply
articles.authors: James Goadsby, Argyro Syngelaki, Laura A Magee, Peter von Dadelszen, David Wright, Kypros H Nicolaides
articles.summary: David Baud and colleagues are right to consider context and how their clinical setting compares with that where the trial was done. However, we maintain that personalised timing of birth for women at increased risk of pre-eclampsia is an intervention that identifies those who might benefit most, and do, by a 30% reduction in term pre-eclampsia, without an increase in caesarean birth or neonatal unit admission.1

articles.title: [Correspondence] Meta-analyses of biased RCTs give biased results, even on individual data
articles.authors: François Gueyffier, Jim M Wright, Rémy Boussageon, Guillaume Grenet
articles.summary: We strongly disagree with Guo and colleagues,1 who suggest an overall benefit from blood pressure targets of less than 120 or 130 mm Hg. It would be surprising for this conclusion to be true given that there is currently no randomised controlled trial (RCT) evidence demonstrating the benefit of blood pressure lowering drugs (BPLD) in mild hypertension (untreated systolic blood pressure 140–160 mm Hg).2

articles.title: [Correspondence] Meta-analyses of biased RCTs give biased results, even on individual data – Authors' reply
articles.authors: Yingxian Sun, Jing Li
articles.summary: We thank François Gueyffier and colleagues for their thoughtful comments on our Article.1 We believe that academic discussions are essential for advancing knowledge, and their feedback is of great importance to us. We welcome the opportunity to engage in further academic exchange.

articles.title: [Editorial] The future of preconception health
articles.authors: The Lancet
articles.summary: In 2018, the first Lancet Series on preconception health, led by Judith Stephenson, argued that the health and nutrition of both men and women before conception is important not only for pregnancy outcomes but also for the lifelong health of their children. We called for a campaign built on a three-pronged approach that recognised the biological, individual, and public health dimensions of preconception, taking a life course view. 8 years on, that call has been only partially answered. The Series helped to broaden the concept of preconception health, beyond a narrow focus on women's health before pregnancy, to encompass the health and wellbeing of women, men, and couples before conception.

articles.title: [Comment] Liver disease: screening for the elusive adversary
articles.authors: Kushala W M Abeysekera, Ryan M Buchanan
articles.summary: It is almost 60 years since the original Wilson and Jungner1 criteria for evaluating population screening programmes were published, with the tenets presented having stood the test of time. Liver disease is a curious case in which to apply these principles. Supporting the rationale for screening is the long-asymptomatic phase of disease and the very poor outcomes for late presentations. However, there is no consensus about who has a clinically relevant disease and who gets treatment, and the most accurate non-invasive testing approach undermines these justifications.

articles.title: [Comment] Offline: World war—is it too late?
articles.authors: Richard Horton
articles.summary: As you drive out of Geneva towards the airport, along the Rue de Lausanne, you pass a long, flat, ochre-brick building lying between the road and the lake: the World Trade Organization. Fenced off with formidable security, the headquarters of a body charged with administering a rules-based international trade order does not exude welcoming warmth. But it does give a hint of humanity by naming itself on the pillars erected around the building—the Centre William Rappard. Who was William Rappard? He was born in New York in 1883, but moved to Switzerland when he was 17 years old.

articles.title: [World Report] Iranian health facilities come under attack amid mounting obstacles to care
articles.authors: Sharmila Devi
articles.summary: Attacks on hospitals and a communications blackout in Iran threaten access to essential care and complicate the humanitarian response. Sharmila Devi reports.

articles.title: [World Report] How US states are aiming to fill federal health gaps
articles.authors: Roxanne Nelson
articles.summary: As trust in the US federal health agencies drops, states have been creating their own public health networks. Roxanne Nelson reports.

articles.title: [Perspectives] Mammography should include artificial intelligence support
articles.authors: Eric J Topol
articles.summary: In the past decade, deep learning has increasingly been applied to medical image interpretation, but without consensus as to whether medical practice should change. Many studies show artificial intelligence (AI) enhanced accuracy beyond the performance of radiologists. The most compelling evidence comes from the follow-up of the Mammography Screening with Artificial Intelligence (MASAI) randomised trial, which assessed the reading of two radiologists compared with one radiologist plus an AI algorithm.

articles.title: [Perspectives] The Pitt: essential work
articles.authors: Anna Reisman
articles.summary: The television series The Pitt, currently in its second season, brilliantly captures the emergency room (ER) of the fictional Pittsburgh Trauma Medical Hospital in all its stressful, raw, and deeply human complexity. Each season represents one intense day in the ER; each episode captures 1 hour during a single day shift, bookended by the arrival of a fresh-faced night team. The series centres on chief ER physician Michael Robinavitch (Dr Robby), played by Noah Wyle, along with his team of nurses, residents, students, and staff, and the patients they encounter.

articles.title: [Perspectives] On boundaries and being a medical relative
articles.authors: Rageshri Dhairyawan
articles.summary: The cheers of the home crowd escaped the hospital room through open windows. “Another four!”, my father cried in frustration. England had scored yet another boundary against what was proving to be a hapless Indian side. I spent many hours last summer watching cricket on a tiny pay-per-view television screen at my father's bedside after he had been admitted to hospital. Watching cricket together was a familiar childhood experience, but this was a new situation—my first time as a medical relative.

articles.title: [Obituary] John Bertrand Gurdon
articles.authors: Talha Burki
articles.summary: Developmental biologist who won the 2012 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine. Born on Oct 2, 1933 in Dippenhall, UK, he died on Oct 7, 2025, in Hardingham, UK, aged 92 years.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Contemporary non-invasive imaging for coronary artery disease
articles.authors: Haoyi Zheng
articles.summary: Pieter van der Bijl and colleagues provide an excellent and timely Review of cardiac imaging in coronary artery disease.1 Several statements, however, merit clarification to avoid misinterpretation in clinical practice.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Contemporary non-invasive imaging for coronary artery disease
articles.authors: Imre Hunyor, Ian Wilcox, John O'Sullivan
articles.summary: Pieter van der Bijl and colleagues1 provide an excellent overview of contemporary imaging in chronic coronary artery disease, including the advantages of photon-counting CT. Their Review notes perivascular fat attenuation as a marker of vascular inflammation. We write to highlight an important distinction: the difference between raw perivascular fat attenuation index measurements and the standardised, percentile-based fat attenuation index score now used in clinical prognostic tools.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Contemporary non-invasive imaging for coronary artery disease – Authors' reply
articles.authors: Pieter van der Bijl, Jeroen J Bax
articles.summary: We thank Haoyi Zheng for his interest in our Review of the contemporary, non-invasive imaging diagnosis of chronic coronary artery disease.1 Angina with non-obstructive coronary arteries (ANOCA) or ischaemia with no obstructive coronary artery disease (INOCA) are important causes of chest pain. In the Inclusive Invasive Physiological Assessment in Angina Syndromes registry, ANOCA accounted for 20% of patients referred for invasive coronary angiography after a positive stress test.2 ANOCA and INOCA not only impair quality of life but also increase mortality.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Unclear benefits of higher doses of influenza vaccine
articles.authors: Antoine Flahault
articles.summary: I read with interest the pooled analysis of two large pragmatic randomised clinical trials reported by Niklas Dyrby Johansen and colleagues.1 Based on the comparison of high-dose and standard-dose influenza vaccines randomly allocated to 465 400 participants aged 65 years and older, the authors concluded that the high-dose vaccine had “demonstrated superior protection” compared with standard-dose vaccines against hospitalisations for influenza or pneumonia, and other causes.1

articles.title: [Correspondence] Unclear benefits of higher doses of influenza vaccine – Authors' reply
articles.authors: Niklas Dyrby Johansen, Jacobo Pardo- Seco, Federico Martinón-Torres, Tor Biering-Sørensen
articles.summary: We appreciate the interest in our Article on the FLUNITY-HD1 trial and the opportunity to clarify these points. As the DANFLU-22 and GALFLU3 trials were indeed prospectively harmonised using the same protocol framework, endpoint hierarchy, and ICD 10th edition-based variable definitions, an individual-level pooled analysis was a priori found to be the most suitable and statistically efficient way of analysing the collective data. It can reasonably be argued that the FLUNITY-HD1 trial more closely resembles a single pragmatic trial conducted across two sites than a conventional individual participant data meta-analysis of independent studies.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Methoxyflurane: analgesic benefit and environmental impact
articles.authors: Chika Edward Uzoigwe, Frederick Campbell-Jones
articles.summary: The UK National Health Service and Norwegian health-care system have set targets to achieve carbon neutrality by 2040 and 2050, respectively. With this goal in mind, it is necessary to consider the environmental impact of new medical interventions, notwithstanding the fact that patient care is the ultimate determinant of any therapeutic option. As carbon emission reduction strategies are implemented, any new greenhouse gas contributions will need to be carefully recorded. In their innovative study, Randi Simensen and colleagues explore the effectiveness of the inhalational agent methoxyflurane in a prehospital setting and show high analgesic efficacy.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Methoxyflurane: analgesic benefit and environmental impact – Authors' reply
articles.authors: Randi Simensen, Marius Rehn, Jostein Hagemo, Lars Olav Fjose, Fridtjof Heyerdahl
articles.summary: We thank Chika Edward Uzoigwe and Frederick Campbell-Jones for their thoughtful comments and for highlighting the importance of environmental responsibility. Climate impact should indeed be integrated in evaluations of novel treatment modalities and aligned with improvements in clinical quality and patient outcomes. However, a balanced assessment requires realistic assumptions about the frequency of use and a sound perspective of impact.

articles.title: [Articles] Prevalence of liver fibrosis in the general population (the LiverScreen project): a multinational European cohort study
articles.authors: Isabel Graupera, Maja Thiele, Laurent Castera, Guillem Pera, Salvatore Piano, Anna Sòria, Núria Fabrellas, Pere Toran, Carla Chacon, Katrine T Bech, Helle Lindholm Schnefeld, Marta Tonon, Simone Incicco, Joël Moussy, Vincent Lévy, Anita Madir, Sandro Kukic, Daniel Jan Havaj, Svetlana Adamcova-Selcanova, Jesse Pustjens, Laurens A van Kleef, Alba Jiménez-Masip, Laura Pagès, Mirko Zoncapè, Susanne N Weber, Peter R Galle, Rebecca Harris, Luis Ibáñez-Samaniego, Rosa M Morillas, Alba Diaz, Sönke Detlefsen, Miquel Serra-Burriel, Anita Arslanow, Peter Andersen, Judit Pich, Eva Bonfill, Marko Korenjak, Céline Fournier-Poizat, Anne Llorca, Marie-Caroline Gourmelon, Harry J de Koning, Martina Pérez-Guasch, Ann Thu Ma, Adrià Juanola, Elisa Pose, Ingrid Arteaga, Ida Villesen, Johanne Kragh Hansen, Valeria Calvino, Roberta Gagliardi, Bahija Boutouria, Frane Pastrovic, Petra Dinjar Kujundzic, Daniela Zilincanova, Karolina Kristina Sulejova, Diego Rojo, Robert J de Knegt, Maykon Diego Melo, Antonio Torrejón, Rosario Hernández-Ibáñez, Jordi Hoyo, Laura Muñoz, Raquel López-Martos, Simon J Griffin, Michael Manns, Tom H Karlsen, Phil N Newsome, Patrick S Kamath, Rafael Bañares, Indra N Guha, Jörn M Schattenberg, Frank Lammert, Emmanuel Tsochatzis, Willem P Brouwer, Juan M Pericàs, Lubomir Skladany, Ivica Grgurevic, Dominique Roulot, Paolo Angeli, Aleksander Krag, Llorenç Caballeria, Pere Ginès, LiverScreen Consortium Investigators
articles.summary: Undiagnosed liver fibrosis is common in the general population in Europe and is primarily driven by metabolic factors and alcohol consumption. Early detection is pivotal as it could allow personalised interventions that can prevent progression to cirrhosis and complications.

articles.title: [Editorial] Childhood cancer: progress, but not enough
articles.authors: The Lancet
articles.summary: The death of a child has profound and lasting emotional consequences for families, underscoring the importance of improving survival for children with cancer. According to the CONCORD-4 study, published in The Lancet, many countries are on track not just to achieve, but to surpass, the 2018 WHO Global Initiative for Childhood Cancer target of 60% survival at 5 years for all childhood cancers by 2030. Yet this achievement masks data gaps and deep disparities in cancer care worldwide, with children in the poorest countries likely to face far lower survival rates.

articles.title: [Comment] Childhood cancer: an equity test for global health
articles.authors: Julia E Heck, Wafaa M Rashed, Anupong Sirirungreung, Juan Manuel Mejia-Arangure
articles.summary: The incidence of paediatric cancers has been difficult to assess, due both to the nature of these cancers and to inadequate data availability. Globally, only 21% of the population lives in areas with population-based cancer registries, some with varying data quality or passive follow-up; 38% of the global population lives in areas with death registration.1,2 Furthermore, childhood cancers are best classified by their morphology rather than by their site, necessitating pathological review for accurate diagnosis.

articles.title: [Comment] 10 years after NOBLE: more nuance in left main revascularisation
articles.authors: Kuniaki Takahashi, William F Fearon
articles.summary: Unprotected left main coronary artery disease is one of the most studied anatomical subsets in coronary revascularisation. Current guidelines from the USA and Europe endorse coronary artery bypass grafting (CABG) as the preferred revascularisation strategy over percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) for patients with left main coronary artery disease, as CABG has shown reductions in myocardial infarction, and repeat revascularisation, although a consistent mortality benefit has not been shown.

articles.title: [Comment] Offline: The scandal of missing mothers
articles.authors: Richard Horton
articles.summary: Amartya Sen, Nobel laureate in economics and a champion of the idea that human development is the progressive acquisition of freedoms and capabilities, long ago identified a neglected injustice in global affairs—a deficit of women relative to men. He called them the “missing women”. There were multiple reasons to explain why these women went missing: lack of medical care, sex-selective abortion, and pervasive gender inequality. In 1990, Sen estimated that as many as 100 million women might be missing.

articles.title: [World Report] Are India and China ready for a “chaotic surge” of generic obesity drugs?
articles.authors: Megan Tatum
articles.summary: As patents on semaglutide expire, manufacturers are poised to introduce cheaper generic versions of blockbuster drugs, with far-reaching effects on the obesity crisis. Megan Tatum reports.

articles.title: [Perspectives] Nina Schwalbe: global health advocate running for US Congress
articles.authors: Faith McLellan
articles.summary: Global health expert and advocate Nina R Schwalbe has spent her career working on multifaceted global health issues, ranging from HIV and tuberculosis prevention to negotiations for a pandemic treaty. But in 2026 she has entered another complex and fraught arena, that of national politics, running for the US Congress as a Democratic candidate for the US House of Representatives from New York's 12th Congressional District, where her family has lived for six generations. The list of contenders is long and includes Jack Schlossberg, the grandson of former US President John F Kennedy.

articles.title: [Perspectives] Still dancing in defiance
articles.authors: Sophie Harman
articles.summary: The spring morning in 2026 was not the first time I had seen Tracey Emin's 1995 work Why I Never Became a Dancer but it was the first time it made me cry. The short 7-minute film tells the story of sex between 13-year-old Emin and older men, slut shaming, thwarted ambition, escape, and reclaiming joy and your narrative.

articles.title: [Perspectives] Social habitus: a factory worker in a stroke rehabilitation unit in France
articles.authors: Muriel Darmon, Mayssa Rekhis
articles.summary: Mr K, a factory worker aged 50 years, was admitted to the inpatient rehabilitation unit of a university hospital in France after initial treatment of a frontal lobe stroke in the hospital's neurology unit. The team prescribed physiotherapy, occupational therapy, and speech therapy.

articles.title: [Obituary] Thomas Fogarty
articles.authors: Andrew Green
articles.summary: Cardiovascular surgeon who invented the Fogarty catheter and was a pioneer in minimally invasive surgery. Born on Feb 25, 1934, in Cincinnati, OH, USA, he died on Dec 28, 2025, in Los Altos, CA, USA, aged 91 years.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Clinical phenotyping improves choices of biologics in asthma
articles.authors: Katrin Milger, Johann Christian Virchow, Marek Lommatzsch
articles.summary: We read with interest the Series paper by Elliot Israel and colleagues on current biologic treatment options in severe asthma.1 The authors use blood eosinophil count thresholds—in addition to assessing comorbidities—as the main basis for their algorithm to select a targeted biologic treatment. Furthermore, they compare the efficacy of biologics in clinical trials based solely on a blood eosinophil count threshold of 300 or more eosinophils per μL. However, using blood eosinophil thresholds alone to compare biological efficacy falls short of the fact that the clinical significance of blood eosinophils depends strongly on the clinical asthma phenotype,2 which varies among the populations in the reported trials.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Clinical phenotyping improves choices of biologics in asthma – Authors’ reply
articles.authors: Elliot Israel, David J Jackson, Michael E Wechsler, Wendy C Moore
articles.summary: We thank Katrin Milger and colleagues for their thoughtful comments on our Series paper on anti-cytokine biologics for severe asthma.1 We agree that blood eosinophils should not be used in isolation and that multicomponent phenotyping—including age of onset, allergic status, biomarkers, and comorbidities—can help identify patients more likely to benefit from biologic therapy. However, the strongest predictor of response remains the baseline level of type 2 inflammation.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Perioperative immunotherapy for resectable hepatocellular carcinoma
articles.authors: Zhenli Li, Yufu Tang
articles.summary: We read with great interest the Article by Zheng Wang and colleagues,1 and appreciate their novel findings. The study, centred around surgery, proposed a novel treatment of neoadjuvant therapy, radical surgery, and adjuvant therapy, with camrelizumab plus rivoceranib, in patients with resectable hepatocellular carcinoma at intermediate or high risk of recurrence. The trial showed that, compared with surgery alone, this approach significantly improved the median event-free survival in the selected population (42·1 months [95% CI 23·2 to not estimable] vs 19·4 months [14·9 to not estimable]).

articles.title: [Correspondence] Perioperative immunotherapy for resectable hepatocellular carcinoma
articles.authors: Wenyi Jin, Mingyang Xue, Susu Luo
articles.summary: The CARES-009 trial showed that perioperative camrelizumab plus rivoceranib significantly improves event-free survival compared with surgery alone in patients with resectable hepatocellular carcinoma (42·1 months [95% CI 23·2 to not estimable] vs 19·4 months [14·9 to not estimable]).1 While these findings represent an important advance, several methodological concerns warrant further discussion.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Perioperative immunotherapy for resectable hepatocellular carcinoma – Authors’ reply
articles.authors: Fei Liang, Shaolai Zhou, Zheng Wang, Jia Fan, Jian Zhou, the CARES-009 investigators
articles.summary: We read the Correspondence by Zhenli Lu and Yufu Tang in response to our Article,1 and welcome the opportunity to respond. The discrepancy in hepatocellular carcinoma resectability criteria reflects inherent regional variations in health-care infrastructure, epidemiological profiles, and therapeutic frameworks. Recent updates to major international guidelines (eg, the Japan Society of Hepatology's 2021 version,2 the Korean Liver Cancer Association's 2022 version,3 and the American Association for the Study of Liver Diseases’ 2023 version4) are actively re-evaluating surgery-centred multidisciplinary management for advanced disease—an alignment that reinforces our study's conceptual framework.

articles.title: [Articles] Global burden of cancer in children and adolescents aged 0–19 years, 1990–2023: a systematic analysis for the Global Burden of Disease Study 2023
articles.authors: GBD 2023 Childhood Cancer Collaborators
articles.summary: Global childhood cancer burden remains a substantial contributor to global childhood disease and cancer burden and is disproportionately weighted towards resource-limited settings. The estimation of additional cancer types relevant in childhood provides a step towards alignment with WHO GICC targets. Efforts to decrease global childhood cancer burden should focus on addressing the inequities in burden worldwide and support comprehensive improvements along the childhood cancer diagnosis and care continuum.

articles.title: [Articles] Percutaneous coronary intervention versus coronary artery bypass grafting for unprotected left main stenosis: 10-year final results from the randomised, open-label, non-inferiority NOBLE trial
articles.authors: Emil Nielsen Holck, Niels R Holm, David Hildick-Smith, M Mitchell Lindsay, Mark S Spence, Andrejs Erglis, Ian B A Menown, Terje Steigen, Lone Juul Hune Mogensen, Ivy Susanne Modrau, Matti Niemelä, Jens F Lassen, Keith Oldroyd, Peteris Stradins, Simon J Walsh, Petter C Endresen, Thor Trovik, Ole Fröbert, Alastair N J Graham, Vesa Anttila, Uday Trivedi, Leif Thuesen, Evald H Christiansen
articles.summary: There was no significant difference in all-cause mortality at 10 years between PCI and CABG for patients with unprotected left main coronary artery disease and no additional complex lesions, indicating that PCI is equally as safe as CABG in patients eligible for both treatments. These results will aid heart teams in developing an individualised patient-centred strategy.

articles.title: Newly found macrophages stand guard inside blood vessels
articles.summary: Nature Immunology, Published online: 03 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41590-026-02503-9Using high-resolution live imaging in zebrafish, we discovered a population of macrophages residing within blood vessels, termed blood vessel-resident macrophages. These cells clear foreign particles and unfit cells from the circulation and rapidly respond to endothelial injury. Blood vessel-resident macrophages therefore constitute a dedicated immune population that safeguards blood and vascular integrity.

articles.title: Engineering metabolite-sensing NK and T cells to target solid tumors
articles.summary: Nature Immunology, Published online: 03 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41590-026-02500-yThrough CRISPR activation screens, we discovered that metabolite-sensing receptors can be used to mobilize natural killer cells and T cells toward solid tumors. Aberrant tumor metabolism is routinely used for diagnosis. Our work now shows that tumor metabolism can be leveraged for therapeutics through the design of tumor-targeted metabolite-sensing effector immune cells.

articles.title: How procrastination can rob you of career fulfilment in science

articles.title: [Editorial] Politicisation of the US FDA: eroding integrity and trust
articles.authors: The Lancet
articles.summary: The principle that medicines should not reach patients without strong safety guardrails forms the bedrock of the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA). The FDA shapes decisions about which medicines reach consumers around the world, through robust standards for safety, efficacy, and post-marketing oversight in drug regulatory practices, with a 2026 budget of $6·8 billion. It faces a tough balancing act in maintaining scientific standards and rigorous review while bringing medicines and devices to the patients who need them as quickly as possible.

articles.title: [Comment] New hope for neurotrophin targeting in osteoarthritis pain?
articles.authors: Tonia L Vincent
articles.summary: Despite its enormous global burden, osteoarthritis has no licensed disease-modifying treatments, and available analgesics have moderate efficacy and substantial side-effects.1 The success of neutralising antibodies to nerve growth factor (NGF) for osteoarthritis pain in 20102 could arguably have been described as the most important clinical advance in osteoarthritis since surgical joint replacement in the 1960s. Not only did the anti-NGF monoclonal antibody show a level of analgesia which surpassed all licensed osteoarthritis pain medications, but it also led to a greater understanding of the pathophysiology of osteoarthritis pain.

articles.title: [Comment] Thank you to The Lancet's reviewers in 2025
articles.authors: The Editors of The Lancet
articles.summary: 2025 was a turbulent year in medicine and health research globally.1 Attacks on equity, diversity, and inclusion (EDI) policies initiated by the administration of US President Donald Trump; a wave of terminations and resignations at US agencies and the National Institutes of Health; and withdrawal of the USA from WHO all led to repercussions felt by the research and academic publishing communities far beyond the US border. And continued efforts to address research integrity issues, fuelled partly by paper mill activities and inappropriate creation of AI-generated content, resulted in a series of retractions across multiple journals worldwide.

articles.title: [Comment] Offline: Medical culture needs reform
articles.authors: Richard Horton
articles.summary: Age may not bring wisdom, but it does allow one to contrast the extraordinary differences between times past and times present. In 1986, the year I qualified in medicine, no one talked about culture. We were thrown onto the wards with, if you were lucky, a few weeks of hasty experience as a locum house officer. Those early days were spent perfecting the ability to insert intravenous lines into extremely pain-tolerant patients. Or making sure a bottle of champagne was appropriately chilled for the end of the Friday afternoon varicose vein clinic (I joke not).

articles.title: [Perspectives] Arghavan Salles: seeing the value of diversity, equity, and inclusion
articles.authors: Aarathi Prasad
articles.summary: “My mom and I left Iran when I was 5 years old, just the two of us…there had been the revolution in 1979, so it was a combination of factors, wanting to get her PhD, but also wanting to get away from that regime”, says Arghavan Salles, Clinical Associate Professor and Special Advisor for Culture and Community Building in the Department of Medicine, and a Senior Research Scholar at the Clayman Institute for Gender Research, both at Stanford University, CA, USA. That was in 1985. After moving around for a while, the two ended up in Iowa.

articles.title: [Perspectives] Face, identity, and culture
articles.authors: Gabriel Weston
articles.summary: Fay Bound-Alberti, writer and Professor in Modern History at King's College London, UK, was 5 years into an ambitious research project examining the interdisciplinary meanings of the face when she suddenly realised she had been suffering all her life from a rare condition called prosopagnosia, or face blindness. A poignant passage in her new book The Face: A Cultural History describes how she went to collect her daughter from nursery only to discover she could not distinguish her among the crowd of other toddlers.

articles.title: [Obituary] Alan R Hinman
articles.authors: Faith McLellan
articles.summary: Epidemiologist and public health leader in immunisation practices and disease prevention. Born on March 23, 1937, in New Orleans, LA, USA, he died after a brief illness in Decatur, GA, USA, on Jan 26, 2026, aged 88 years.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Spaceflight medical risk management: NASA's Crew-11 mission
articles.authors: Farhan M Asrar, Barry Wilmore
articles.summary: For the first time in the history of both the National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) and the International Space Station, a crew medical concern prompted an unprecedented evacuation, ending Crew-11's mission early.1 This event raised questions about the rationale for the decision and whether it constituted a mission failure.

articles.title: [Correspondence] African Union's Common Position on NCDs, injuries, and mental health
articles.authors: Manuel Kassaye Sibhatu, Abdulaziz Mohammed, Adelard Kakunze, Bethlehem Arega, Jean Kaseya
articles.summary: The African Union, through the Africa Centres for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), has operationalised strategies on non-communicable diseases (NCDs), injuries, and mental health to reverse the substantial human and economic loss from these conditions.1 In Africa, cardiovascular diseases and other NCDs account for over 70% of the region's premature deaths.5 The occurrence of road traffic injuries (26·6 deaths per 100 000 population) is increasing compared with previous years, and mental health conditions now affect 14% of the population.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Think beyond the standard: anti-MRSA options in community-acquired pneumonia
articles.authors: Alberto Enrico Maraolo, Nicola Schiano Moriello, Ivan Gentile
articles.summary: Luis Felipe Reyes and colleagues’ recent Seminar1 on community-acquired pneumonia offers a timely synthesis of advancements in diagnosis and management of the infection. However, the section on treatment does not comprehensively address the role of novel agents specifically approved for meticillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA). Only vancomycin and linezolid are mentioned as options for patients with severe infection.1

articles.title: [Correspondence] Think beyond the standard: anti-MRSA options in community-acquired pneumonia – Authors' reply
articles.authors: Luis Felipe Reyes, Andrew Conway Morris, Cristian Serrano-Mayorga, Lennie P G Derde, Robert P Dickson, Ignacio Martin-Loeches
articles.summary: We thank Alberto Enrico Maraolo and colleagues for their careful reading of our Seminar1 on community-acquired pneumonia and for raising an important point: the expanding therapeutic armamentarium against meticillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA). However, this increase in treatment options does not change the core principle that empirical MRSA coverage should be reserved for patients with clearly defined risk factors or confirmed infection.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Unanswered questions regarding the AIR strategy in children – Authors' reply
articles.authors: Lee Hatter, Karen Oldfield, Mark Weatherall, Andrew Bush, Richard Beasley
articles.summary: We thank Stéphanie Lejeune and colleagues for their insightful comments on the Article1 reporting the CARE study, and welcome the opportunity to clarify the points raised.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Complement biomarkers during iptacopan treatment
articles.authors: Felix Poppelaars, V Michael Holers, Joshua M Thurman
articles.summary: We read with interest the Article1 by David Kavanagh and colleagues showing that the complement factor B inhibitor iptacopan significantly reduces proteinuria in patients with C3 glomerulopathy.1 With its approval, the reach of complement inhibitors continues to expand, underscoring their emerging role in diseases previously considered not complement-mediated or untreatable.2 While the authors commendably included complement biomarkers and repeat biopsies, they failed to comprehensively examine their value for monitoring and guiding therapy.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Complement biomarkers during iptacopan treatment – Authors' reply
articles.authors: David Kavanagh, Andrew S Bomback, Marina Vivarelli, Carla M Nester, Richard J H Smith
articles.summary: We appreciate the thoughtful commentary on our Article by Felix Poppelaars and colleagues, and their emphasis on complement biomarker interpretation in C3 glomerulopathy. Here, we would like to clarify several points and provide context from the APPEAR-C3G trial.1

articles.title: The DNA virome varies with human genes and environments
articles.authors: Nolan Kamitaki, David Tang, Steven A. McCarroll, Po-Ru Loh
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10288-yAnalyses of biobank data show that human variation such as age, sex and genetics, particularly at the major histocompatibility complex locus, is associated with viral abundance and supports a causal link between abundance of Epstein–Barr virus and Hodgkin’s lymphoma.

articles.title: Rapid concerted switching of the neural code in the inferotemporal cortex
articles.authors: Yuelin Shi, Dasheng Bi, Janis K. Hesse, Frank F. Lanfranchi, Shi Chen, Doris Y. Tsao
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10267-3Face cells in the macaque inferotemporal cortex are initially able to detect faces and then rapidly switch to a face-specific neural code to discriminate between different face identities.

articles.title: Yin and yang of tumor-associated macrophages
articles.summary: Nature Immunology, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41590-026-02486-7Our work reveals functional heterogeneity among interstitial macrophages, showing that distinct chemokine programs determine immune cell positioning and interactions within lung tumors. Interstitial macrophage niches support anti-tumor tertiary lymphoid structure formation and lymphocyte recruitment, but they also recruit pro-tumorigenic macrophages to the tumor microenvironment. Moreover, monocyte-derived dendritic cells migrate to tumor-draining lymph nodes, where they prime immunosuppressive responses. Blockade of this migration improved dendritic cell-based vaccination.

articles.title: CD4+ T cell-mediated immunity protects from VSV-SUD lethal challenge in a mouse model of Sudan virus infection
articles.authors: Lara Kelchtermans, Katrien Geerts, Cuong Thi Pham, Vanshika Malviya, Winston Chiu, Carolien De Keyzer, Elias Broeckhoven, Johan Neyts, Susan Schlenner, Yeranddy A. Alpizar, Kai Dallmeier
articles.summary: Nature Immunology, Published online: 25 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41590-026-02478-7In a mouse model that mimics Sudan virus infection, a deadly relative of Ebola virus, vaccine protection depends on virus-specific CD4 T cells that coordinate antiviral defenses, even in the absence of neutralizing antibodies.

articles.title: Commensals join the effector game
articles.authors: Julia Sanchez-Garrido
articles.summary: Nature Immunology, Published online: 24 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41590-026-02467-wBacterial type III secretion systems, traditionally viewed as virulence factors, are prevalent in healthy gut microbiomes, where they deliver effectors that modulate human immune signaling and influence susceptibility to inflammatory diseases.

articles.title: MHC class I on target cells regulates CD4+ T cell-mediated immunity
articles.authors: Emma Lauder, Mahnoor Gondal, Meng-Chih Wu, Akira Yamamoto, Laure Maneix, Dongchang Zhao, Yaping Sun, Marcin Cieslik, Arul M. Chinnaiyan, Pavan Reddy
articles.summary: Nature Immunology, Published online: 24 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41590-026-02480-zHere, the authors suggest that, when major histocompatibility complex class I is downregulated on allogenic or tumor cells, they are more susceptible to CD4+ T cell-mediated ferroptosis.

articles.title: Chemokine-defined macrophage niches establish spatial organization of tumor immunity
articles.authors: Soubhik Ghosh, Xin Li, Kavita Rawat, Aishwarya Dighal, Stephanie Kalinowski, Reza Hosseini, Fred W. Kolling, Carol S. Ringelberg, Claudia V. Jakubzick
articles.summary: Nature Immunology, Published online: 23 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41590-026-02445-2Jakubzick and colleagues show that the tumor microenvironment is shaped by opposing programs in tissue-resident CD206hi and CD206lo interstitial macrophages and Ly6c2+Fn1+Vcan+ recruited macrophages, various macrophage subsets that are spatially segregated and mechanistically distinct.

articles.title: Engineering NK and T cells with metabolite-sensing receptors to target solid tumors

articles.summary: Nature Immunology, Published online: 23 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41590-026-02473-yHere the authors use CRISPR activation screens to identify metabolite-sensing receptors that affect lymphocyte targeting and infiltration of tumors and engineer CAR T and CAR NK cells with these receptors to enhance solid tumor killing in mice.

articles.title: [Editorial] Making treatment for obesity more equitable
articles.authors: The Lancet
articles.summary: 2026 may prove a pivotal year for obesity. GLP-1 receptor agonists have revolutionised obesity management over the past 10 years. Mounting evidence has established their benefits across many cardiometabolic and obesity-related conditions and the global weight-loss medications market is predicted to reach US$150 billion by 2035. More than a billion people live with obesity, with the burden rising rapidly in low-income and middle-income countries. High costs, limited production capacity, and supply-chain constraints have resulted in persistent inequitable access to GLP-1 receptor agonists.

articles.title: [Comment] Physiologically guided CABG in valve surgery
articles.authors: Sachin S Goel, Michael J Reardon
articles.summary: Physiological evaluation using fractional flow reserve (FFR) provides more accurate assessment of the haemodynamic significance of coronary artery stenosis than coronary angiography alone. FFR-guided percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) results in better short-term and long-term outcomes than PCI guided by coronary angiography or medical therapy alone.1,2 Angiography-derived FFR is a way to estimate FFR from standard coronary angiography images using computational fluid dynamics or related algorithms, without passing a pressure wire or administering hyperaemic agents, with overall good correlation with invasive, wire-based FFR.

articles.title: [Comment] Should we keep pushing a high fluid intake in kidney stones?
articles.authors: Kelly Lambert, Hicham Ibrahim Cheikh Hassan
articles.summary: Urinary stone disease is common, associated with substantial morbidity, has a high recurrence rate, and affects quality of life.1,2 Consumption of large volumes of fluids is a foundational strategy for preventing stone formation,3 but adherence rates to this advice have been difficult to achieve in routine care.4 Patients frequently report practical, behavioural, and environmental barriers that hinder sustained behaviour change,5,6 and evidence regarding strategies to support long-term adherence have been scarce.

articles.title: [Comment] Surgical approach matters for long-term lung cancer outcomes
articles.authors: Olaf Mercier
articles.summary: For more than a decade, the medical community has sought to ascertain the oncological effect of perioperative management during cancer surgery.1 Despite encouraging preclinical study results showing that the choice of drugs used during or after surgery and the degree of invasiveness of the surgery could increase the risk of tumour recurrence, no clinical trials have succeeded in translating these results into clinical practice.2,3 This is particularly true for thoracic surgery, a specialty that has witnessed major advances in lung cancer management over recent years with the development of minimally invasive surgical techniques (eg, video-assisted, robotic, uniportal, and monoportal approaches), enhanced post-surgical rehabilitation programmes, and multimodal treatments including neoadjuvant immunotherapy.

articles.title: [Comment] Offline: Intelligence does not prevent stupidity
articles.authors: Richard Horton
articles.summary: In The Wizard of the Kremlin (2022), Giuliano da Empoli imagines a former political advisor to Vladimir Putin recounting the rise of his new tsar from spook to dictator. (Before becoming a writer, da Empoli was himself a political advisor—to Italy's Prime Minister Matteo Renzi.) His principal character, Vadim Baranov, contrasts the West, where “your society is based on the principle that nothing is greater than money”, with Russia, where the people sought “someone at the top who could guarantee order”.

articles.title: [Perspectives] Claire Calderwood: integrated health screening for tuberculosis
articles.authors: Udani Samarasekera
articles.summary: “Respiratory medicine is deeply connected to social and structural determinants of health in terms of the diseases that we try to prevent and we treat. I think that connection to people's lives and society more generally really appealed to me—it's tangible and as respiratory physicians we have an obligation to be advocates as well as clinicians”, says Claire Calderwood, Academic Clinical Lecturer in Respiratory Medicine at the National Heart and Lung Institute, Imperial College London, UK, and Postdoctoral Researcher at The Health Research Unit Zimbabwe (THRU ZIM), Biomedical Research and Training Institute, Zimbabwe.

articles.title: [Obituary] Katrina Jane Allen
articles.authors: Andrew Green
articles.summary: Paediatric allergist and politician. Born on Feb 24, 1966, in Albury, NSW, Australia, she died of cholangiocarcinoma on Dec 23, 2025, in Melbourne, VIC, aged 59 years.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Closing the adolescent health financing gap: the Global Financing Facility
articles.authors: Emilia Lindquist, Olivia Biermann, Adesola Olumide, Khadija Mitu, Workneh Adebe Yadete, Sara Bylund, Sara Luckenbill, Sarah Baird, Mariam Claeson
articles.summary: Among today's most pressing development challenges is the matching of financing to health needs. One of the critically underfunded global priorities is adolescent health, with adolescents (aged 10–24 years) making up a quarter of the world's population but receiving only 2·4% of development aid for health in 2016–21.1

articles.title: [Correspondence] The need for locally manufactured health commodities in Africa
articles.authors: Jean Kaseya, Noah Takah Fongwen, Yenew Kebede Tebeje, Nebiyu Dereje, Ngashi Ngongo, Shanelle Hall
articles.summary: The 2021 Lancet Commission on diagnostics reported that nearly half of the world's population has limited access to basic diagnostic services.1 Less than 5% of diagnostics and less than 1% of vaccines used in Africa are produced locally.2 Heavy reliance on external supply is a strategic vulnerability, with consequences magnified during pandemics when global competition for medical countermeasures intensifies. Local manufacturing requires more than technical capacity: it demands policies that shape markets, reduce risk for early movers, and reward quality-assured local production.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Safeguarding against dengue fever risks in a more connected world
articles.authors: Emily Ying Yang Chan, Chun-ying Leung, Caroline Dubois, Louise Yung, Zhe Huang
articles.summary: Although malaria elimination has been reported in 14 countries, human health risk reduction against vector-borne diseases remains dire. With neither effective treatment nor widely available prophylaxis, and exacerbated by climate change, urbanisation, and population movements, dengue fever has emerged as a major planetary health threat.1 Currently, vector surveillance, chemical spraying, and biomedically modified vector experimentation are mainstream strategies for addressing mosquito-borne health hazards.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Silence on genocide at the World Conference on Bioethics
articles.authors: Joseph Home, Chantal Patel, Dee Slade
articles.summary: Between Nov 24 and Nov 27, 2025, the International Chair in Bioethics held its 17th World Conference on Bioethics, Medical Ethics, and Health Law in Ljubljana, Slovenia.1 Despite being hosted in a nation whose President has vocally condemned the “genocide in Gaza” at the UN, the conference stood in stark contrast to its setting.2 No scheduled sessions discussed the ongoing humanitarian catastrophe, representing a profound disconnect between the event's location and its content.

articles.title: [Correspondence] On the computability of biological machines
articles.authors: Adrian Woolfson
articles.summary: In the book Natural Theology (1802), the English philosopher and theologian William Paley argued that the complexity of an organism, similar to that of a watch, implied the handiwork of a designer.1 His reasoning rested on the intuition that intricate organisation could not arise from compact generative principles.

articles.title: [Correspondence] Respiratory virus research needs its own PPIE
articles.authors: Daniel Pan, Ji Soo Choi, Mi Joo Choi, Rachael Phillips, Manish Pareek
articles.summary: Patient perspectives are essential alongside clinical and economic evidence in health-care decision making, yet reporting of patient and participant involvement (PPI) in randomised controlled trials remains poor.1 After the COVID-19 pandemic, respiratory virus research in some countries has been deprioritised, with a sense that the public has “already lived through” the pandemic and there is little left to discuss.2,3

articles.title: [Correspondence] PD-L1, treatment intensity, and outcomes in squamous NSCLC
articles.authors: Shujie Huang, Guanqun Yang, Patrick Ming-Kuen Tang
articles.summary: We read with interest the results of the HARMONi-6 trial by Zhiwei Chen and colleagues,1 which showed that ivonescimab plus chemotherapy improved progression-free survival versus tislelizumab plus chemotherapy in advanced squamous non-small-cell lung cancer (NSCLC). However, PD-L1-based subgroup results reveal a critical stratification signal. In patients with PD-L1 tumour proportion score (TPS) of under 50%, dual blockade achieved marked benefit (hazard ratio [HR] 0·63; 95% CI 0·41–0·98), whereas in patients with PD-L1 TPS of 50% or over, the improvement was numerical but not significant (HR 0·71; 0·37–1·33).

articles.title: [Correspondence] PD-L1, treatment intensity, and outcomes in squamous NSCLC
articles.authors: Xiaoling Xu, Wenjing Wang, Huihui Li, Xuefei Hu
articles.summary: We congratulate Zhiwei Chen and colleagues on the important findings of the HARMONi-6 study;1 however, two specific points require clarification to better interpret the findings.

articles.title: [Correspondence] PD-L1, treatment intensity, and outcomes in squamous NSCLC – Authors' reply
articles.authors: Zhiwei Chen, Shun Lu
articles.summary: We appreciate Shujie Huang and colleagues and Xiaoling Xu and colleagues highlighting valuable considerations from the HARMONi-6 trial.1

articles.title: [Articles] Angiography-derived fractional flow reserve versus coronary angiography to guide coronary artery bypass grafting in patients undergoing surgical valve procedures with concomitant coronary artery disease in China (FAVOR IV-QVAS): a multicentre, triple-blind, randomised trial
articles.authors: Yunpeng Zhu, Zhaoyun Cheng, Yuan Zhao, Lin Han, Wei Zhang, Chengxin Zhang, Sumin Yang, Liang Ma, Chenhui Qiao, Zhigang Guo, Dan Zhu, Xiquan Zhang, Liangwan Chen, Zhe Wang, Xiaofeng Ye, Mi Zhou, Haiqing Li, Jiapei Qiu, Hong Xu, Yanjun Sun, Jiaxi Zhu, Zhiyun Xu, Guoyou Qin, William Wijns, Bjorn Redfors, Sigrid Sandner, Mario Gaudino, Shengxian Tu, Qiang Zhao
articles.summary: Among patients undergoing valve surgery with concomitant coronary artery disease, physiologically guided CABG using angiography-derived FFR reduced the incidence of the composite perioperative outcome compared with anatomically guided CABG. These findings support a selective approach to surgical coronary revascularisation guided by physiological assessment in patients undergoing valve procedures.

articles.title: [Articles] Prevention of urinary stones with hydration: a randomised clinical trial of an adherence intervention
articles.authors: Alana C Desai, Naim M Maalouf, Jonathan D Harper, Sri Sivalingam, John C Lieske, H Henry Lai, Peter P Reese, Hunter Wessells, Hongqiu Yang, Hussein R Al-Khalidi, Ziya Kirkali, Gregory E Tasian, Charles D Scales, Urinary Stone Disease Network Investigators
articles.summary: A behavioural intervention programme to promote fluid intake for secondary stone prevention did not reduce recurrent stone events but modestly increased urine volume compared with guideline-based care during a 2-year follow-up period.

articles.title: [Articles] Survival outcome of VATS compared with open lobectomy for lung cancer: an individual patient data meta-analysis of randomised trials
articles.authors: Rosie A Harris, Jacie Jiaqi Law, Long Hao, Dongrong Situ, Finn Amundsen Dittberner, Morten Bendixen, Peter B Licht, Chris A Rogers, Eric Lim
articles.summary: This meta-analysis provides evidence that surgical access by VATS lobectomy improved overall survival compared with open surgery without any compromise to disease-free survival. These results underscore the importance of prioritising VATS when technically feasible as the access of choice for surgical resection of early-stage non-small-cell lung cancer.

articles.title: RNA modifications in gene regulation: Functions and pathways
articles.authors: Jiangbo Wei, Chuan He
articles.summary: RNA is frequently chemically modified, with over 170 types of chemical modifications identified to date in cellular RNAs. These modifications, along with their effector proteins, constitute new layers of gene expression regulation by controlling either the fate of modified RNAs at nearly every stage of their life cycle or local transcription through modulating the nearby chromatin state and transcriptional complexes. This is especially evident in dynamic biological contexts such as cellular state transitions, signaling, immune responses, and stress adaptation.
